# TDM – FDM (Time Division & Frequency Division Multiplexing in Telemetry)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Multiplexing?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplexing** is the process of combining multiple independent message signals (data channels) onto a single transmission channel or communication medium so that they can be transmitted simultaneously to a common destination, where they are subsequently separated (demultiplexed) and routed to their respective receivers.

In telemetry systems, where multiple physical quantities (e.g., temperature, pressure, flow, vibration, RPM) from a remote or inaccessible location (like a satellite, aircraft, or industrial plant) must be conveyed over a **single** radio/data link, multiplexing becomes an absolute necessity. Without multiplexing, each sensor would require its own dedicated transmitter, antenna, and receiver — which is economically, spectrally, and physically impractical.

The two classical analog multiplexing techniques — **FDM** and **TDM** — represent two fundamentally different engineering philosophies:

- **FDM** divides the **frequency spectrum** (horizontal axis) into non-overlapping bands.
- **TDM** divides the **time axis** into non-overlapping slots (vertical slices).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> *"FDM – Subcarrier Generators, Modulators, Bandpass Filters, Practical Considerations, Crosstalk, Guard Bands. TDM – PAM, PAM-PM, PDM, PPM Systems, Signal Generation, Demultiplexing, Synchronization."*

---

### 1.2 Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) — Formal Definition

**Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)** is a multiplexing technique in which multiple independent signals, each occupying a distinct, non-overlapping frequency band within the overall transmission bandwidth, are transmitted simultaneously over a common channel. Each input signal is first translated to a unique carrier frequency (subcarrier) by modulation, ensuring spectral separation; a final carrier then modulates this composite FDM signal for transmission.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a **multi-lane highway** where each lane represents a different frequency band. Cars in Lane 1 can only travel in Lane 1, Cars in Lane 2 only in Lane 2, and so on. Even though all cars are moving *simultaneously* and along the *same physical highway* (the channel), they never collide because each is confined to its own lane. Similarly, in FDM, each message signal gets its own "lane" in the frequency domain, and all messages travel at the same time but in different frequency slots.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea of FDM:** Translate each baseband signal $m_i(t)$ to a different subcarrier frequency $\omega_{ci}$ so that their spectra do not overlap.

---

### 1.3 Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) — Formal Definition

**Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)** is a multiplexing technique in which multiple signals are transmitted over a common channel by *time-sequencing* them — that is, each input signal is allotted a discrete, non-overlapping time slot within a periodic **frame**. Transmission occurs in sequential slices in time, but in a non-overlapping manner so that only one signal occupies the channel at any instant.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of a **single-lane toll booth with multiple windows**. Only one car passes through the single-lane gate at any instant. Window 1 opens for 1 second and lets Car 1 pass, then closes. Window 2 then opens for 1 second and lets Car 2 pass, and so on. Although cars are physically arriving at the same time, they share the single lane by taking turns. **TDM works exactly like this** — each input signal "takes a turn" using the channel for a brief, fixed duration before the next signal gets its slot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea of TDM:** Translate each baseband signal $m_i(t)$ into discrete samples at different instants of time so that they occupy different time slots within a frame.

---

### 1.4 Why Both FDM and TDM Exist — Comparative Snapshot

| Property | FDM | TDM |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Resource Divided | **Frequency spectrum** | **Time slots** |
| Simultaneous? | Yes (parallel in frequency) | No (sequential in time) |
| Crosstalk Mechanism | Spectral overlap | Time-slot overlap |
| Typical Use | Analog telemetry, radio broadcasting | Digital telemetry, PCM |
| Key Component | Subcarrier oscillators + BPF | Commutator / electronic switch |

Standard metrics to remember:
- **Nyquist sampling rate** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ — critical for TDM.
- **Guard band** $f_g$ — unused spectral cushion between adjacent FDM channels.
- **Frame time** $T_f$ — total time to transmit one complete cycle of all channels.
- **Standard IRIG subcarrier frequencies** — **400 Hz, 560 Hz, 730 Hz, …, 165 kHz** (used in FM-FDM telemetry).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Frequency Division Multiplexing — Full System Architecture

### 2.1.1 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

The FDM telemetry system operates in five sequential stages:

**Step 1 — Signal Conditioning (Pre-modulation Filtering):**
Each transducer output (a low-level, often noisy voltage) is amplified and low-pass filtered with a **cutoff frequency $f_m$** to satisfy the Nyquist bandwidth of the measurand.

**Step 2 — Subcarrier Modulation:**
Each conditioned signal $m_i(t)$ modulates a unique subcarrier $c_i(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$ using **AM, FM, or PM**. This shifts the spectrum of $m_i(t)$ to be centered at $\pm f_{ci}$.

- For **AM-DSB-SC subcarrier modulation:**
$$s_i(t) = m_i(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$$

- For **FM subcarrier modulation:**
$$s_i(t) = A \cos\!\left[2\pi f_{ci} t + 2\pi k_f \!\int m_i(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$

**Step 3 — Combining / Summing:**
All subcarrier-modulated signals are linearly summed in a **linear adder** to produce the **composite baseband FDM signal** $s_{FDM}(t)$:
$$s_{FDM}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i(t)$$

**Step 4 — RF Transmission:**
The composite FDM signal modulates an RF carrier $f_{rf}$ (using FM most commonly in telemetry) for radio transmission.

**Step 5 — Demultiplexing (Receiver Side):**
The receiver reverses the process: RF demodulation → subcarrier separation using **bandpass filters (BPFs)** tuned to each $f_{ci}$ → subcarrier demodulation → output reconstruction.

### 2.1.2 Subcarrier Generators

> [!NOTE]
> A **subcarrier generator** is a stable oscillator (typically a Colpitts or Wien-bridge oscillator) producing a sinusoidal waveform at a precise subcarrier frequency $f_{ci}$. In telemetry, subcarrier frequencies follow the **IRIG (Inter-Range Instrumentation Group) standard** for compatibility across platforms.

Common **IRIG proportional-band subcarrier centers:** **400 Hz, 560 Hz, 730 Hz, 960 Hz, 1.3 kHz, 1.7 kHz, 2.3 kHz, 3.0 kHz, 3.9 kHz, 5.4 kHz, 7.35 kHz, 10.5 kHz, 14.5 kHz, 22 kHz, 30 kHz, 40 kHz, 52.5 kHz, 70 kHz, 93 kHz, 124 kHz, 165 kHz**.

### 2.1.3 Modulators

In FDM telemetry, **frequency modulation (FM)** is most commonly used because it offers superior noise immunity (a consequence of Carson's rule bandwidth-vs-SNR tradeoff and the *FM capture effect*). **AM-DSB-SC** is used where simplicity and bandwidth economy matter.

### 2.1.4 Bandpass Filters (BPF)

The **BPF at the receiver** must sharply isolate each subcarrier channel. Specifications:
- **Center frequency** $= f_{ci}$
- **Bandwidth** $= 2 f_{m,i}$ (for DSB) or $2(\Delta f + f_{m,i})$ (for FM)
- **Roll-off** should be steep to minimize adjacent-channel interference.

### 2.1.5 Practical Considerations in FDM

> [!WARNING]
> **Crosstalk in FDM:** Occurs when the sidebands of one channel spill into an adjacent channel due to insufficient filter roll-off or non-linearity in the summing amplifier. Mitigation: **guard bands** $f_g$ and sharp BPFs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Intermodulation Distortion:** When the linear adder is even slightly non-linear, products $f_{ci} \pm f_{cj}$ appear in the spectrum, falling *inside* other channels — impossible to filter out. Mitigation: Use highly linear adders and stay within dynamic range.

**Theoretical vs Practical Bandwidth:**

- **Theoretical (with overlap at 0 Hz):**
$$B_{theory} = N \cdot f_m$$

- **Practical (with guard bands):**
$$B_{practice} = N \cdot f_m + (N-1)\, f_g$$

---

## 2.2 Time Division Multiplexing — Full System Architecture

### 2.2.1 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Low-Pass Filtering:**
Each input signal $m_i(t)$ is bandwidth-limited to $f_{m,i}$ Hz to prevent aliasing.

**Step 2 — Sampling (Nyquist Theorem):**
Each signal is sampled at $f_s \geq 2 f_{m,i}$ samples/second.

**Step 3 — Commutation (Time-Slot Assignment):**
A **commutator** (electromechanical rotating switch historically, or electronic analog switch / multiplexer IC today) sequentially samples each channel once per frame, allocating a short time slot $\tau$ to each.

**Step 4 — Pulse Modulation:**
The sample (a brief voltage pulse) may be pulse-modulated (PAM, PDM, PPM, PCM) before transmission.

**Step 5 — Transmission:**
Pulses are sent sequentially over the channel.

**Step 6 — De-commutation & Reconstruction:**
At the receiver, a synchronized de-commutator routes each slot to the correct output channel, followed by a **low-pass reconstruction filter** (per channel).

### 2.2.2 Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)

> [!NOTE]
> **PAM-TDM** is the simplest TDM form. Each sample's amplitude represents the instantaneous value of $m_i(t)$. PAM may be of two types: **natural sampling** (sample-and-hold where pulse width varies with message) or **flat-top sampling** (constant width, varying amplitude).

### 2.2.3 PAM-PM (PAM with Pulse Modulation)

The composite PAM signal is then modulated onto an RF carrier using AM/FM/PM for radio transmission.

### 2.2.4 Pulse Duration Modulation (PDM) — also called PWM

The sample's *width* varies with $m_i(t)$; amplitude is constant. Robust against amplitude noise. PDM-TDM is widely used in industrial telemetry.

### 2.2.5 Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)

The sample's *position* (time of occurrence within a fixed reference frame) varies with $m_i(t)$; width and amplitude are constant. PPM offers improved noise performance over PAM and PDM.

### 2.2.6 Signal Generation & Demultiplexing

- **Signal Generation:** Achieved by sampling switches (FET/MOSFET-based for high speed) controlled by a sequential ring counter driven by a master clock.
- **Demultiplexing:** Requires a **synchronized commutator** at the receiver — typically driven by a **bit/frame synchronizer** that recovers the clock from a dedicated sync pulse inserted into each frame.

### 2.2.7 Synchronization in TDM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronization** ensures the transmitter commutator and receiver de-commutator are aligned. Without sync, samples route to the wrong output channels. Two types:
> - **Bit synchronization** — aligns within a single slot (clock recovery).
> - **Frame synchronization** — aligns the start of a frame (using a special sync codeword/pulse).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|-------------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz; ensures no aliasing |
| 2 | Frame Time (TDM) | $T_f = N \cdot \tau + T_g$ | $\tau$ = slot width, $T_g$ = guard |
| 3 | Slot Width | $\tau = T_f / N$ | For equal slots |
| 4 | Composite FDM Signal | $s_{FDM}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i(t) \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$ | AM-DSB-SC subcarriers |
| 5 | Practical FDM Bandwidth | $B_{practice} = N f_m + (N-1) f_g$ | With guard band |
| 6 | Theoretical FDM Bandwidth | $B_{theory} = N f_m$ | Without guard band |
| 7 | Carson's Rule (FM subcarrier) | $B = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ | Hz — per channel |
| 8 | PAM Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{m,max}$ | Hz |
| 9 | PDM Pulse Width | $\tau_i = \tau_0 + k \cdot m_i(t)$ | $\tau_0$ = nominal width |
| 10 | PPM Pulse Position | $t_i = t_0 + k_p \cdot m_i(t)$ | $t_0$ = nominal position |
| 11 | IRIG Band Centers | $\approx 400$ Hz to $\approx 165$ kHz | Standard telemetry |
| 12 | Maximum Channels (TDM, no guard) | $N_{max} = \lfloor T_f / \tau_{min} \rfloor$ | $\tau_{min}$ = pulse width |

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of literal `|` inside any derivation — e.g., write $\vert m(t) \vert$ rather than `\|m(t)\|`.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **FDM** dominates in **analog radio broadcasting** (AM/FM broadcast, TV analog), **satellite transponder allocation** (each transponder = an FDM channel), **cable TV**, **aeronautical telemetry (IRIG-106 FM/FM)**, and **SCADA systems over leased analog lines**.
- **TDM** dominates in **digital telephony (T1/E1)**, **digital telemetry (IRIG-106 PCM)**, **spacecraft telemetry**, **industrial data acquisition**, and **modern cellular/wireless baseband processing**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Composite FDM Signal Spectrum

Consider $N$ baseband signals $m_1(t), m_2(t), \ldots, m_N(t)$, each bandlimited to $f_m$ Hz. Each is multiplied (modulated) by a subcarrier $\cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$.

**Step 1 — Single-channel modulated signal:**

$$s_i(t) = m_i(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$$

Using the identity:
$$\cos(A)\cos(B) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\big]$$

The Fourier transform relationship (modulation property):

$$S_i(f) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[M_i(f - f_{ci}) + M_i(f + f_{ci})\big]$$

So the spectrum of $m_i(t)$ — originally occupying $-f_m \le f \le f_m$ — is shifted to be centered around $\pm f_{ci}$, occupying $f_{ci} - f_m \le f \le f_{ci} + f_m$.

**Step 2 — Non-overlap condition:**
For spectral non-overlap:
$$f_{c(i+1)} - f_{ci} \ge 2 f_m + f_g$$

where $f_g$ is the guard band. This ensures BPFs can separate adjacent channels.

**Step 3 — Sum in frequency domain:**

$$S_{FDM}(f) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} S_i(f)$$

In time domain:

$$s_{FDM}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i(t)\cos(2\pi f_{ci} t)$$

**Step 4 — Total bandwidth (with guard bands):**

$$B_{FDM} = N \cdot 2 f_m + (N-1) f_g$$

(The factor of 2 is because DSB spectrum has both USB and LSB.)

For SSB subcarrier modulation, the factor of 2 disappears and:

$$B_{FDM,SSB} = N \cdot f_m + (N-1) f_g$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: TDM Frame Time & Maximum Channel Count

Let there be $N$ input channels, each bandlimited to $f_m$ Hz. Per the Nyquist criterion, each must be sampled at $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

**Step 1 — Slot width determination:**
Each sample occupies a slot of duration $\tau$. The pulse width $\tau$ must be small enough to fit $N$ slots per frame plus any guard interval $T_g$.

$$T_f = N \tau + T_g$$

**Step 2 — Minimum slot width:**
If the source clock is $f_{clk}$ (in pulses/sec), then the absolute minimum slot width is:

$$\tau_{min} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum channels (without guard):**

$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{T_f}{\tau_{min}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor T_f \cdot f_{clk} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 4 — Sampling frequency per channel:**

$$f_s = \frac{1}{T_f}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Nyquist for valid sampling:**

$$f_s = \frac{1}{T_f} \geq 2 f_m$$

Hence:
$$T_f \leq \frac{1}{2 f_m}$$

and the maximum number of channels becomes:

$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{f_{clk}}{2 f_m} \right\rfloor$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — FDM Bandwidth

> **Problem:** A 6-channel FDM telemetry system uses AM-DSB-SC subcarrier modulation. Each input signal is bandlimited to $f_m = 4$ kHz. Subcarrier frequencies are $f_{c1} = 20$ kHz, $f_{c2} = 30$ kHz, …, with a guard band of $f_g = 2$ kHz between adjacent channels. Calculate the total transmission bandwidth.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
$N = 6$, $f_m = 4$ kHz, $f_g = 2$ kHz, modulation = DSB-SC.

**Step 2 — Use the practical DSB FDM bandwidth formula:**

$$B_{FDM} = N \cdot 2 f_m + (N-1) f_g$$

$$B_{FDM} = 6 \cdot 2 \cdot 4 + 5 \cdot 2 \;\; \text{kHz}$$

$$B_{FDM} = 48 + 10 = 58 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Verify by direct spectral accounting:**
The lowest channel spans $20 - 4 = 16$ kHz to $20 + 4 = 24$ kHz.
The highest channel spans $f_{c6} = 20 + 5\cdot 10 = 70$ kHz, spanning $66$ to $74$ kHz.
Total bandwidth $= 74 - 16 = 58$ kHz. ✓

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — TDM Channel Count

> **Problem:** A TDM-PAM system samples 8 analog channels, each with $f_m = 3.5$ kHz. The system clock is 100 kHz and 10% of each frame is guard/sync overhead. Find: (a) minimum sampling rate, (b) frame time, (c) maximum slot width, (d) verify Nyquist.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Minimum sampling rate per channel (Nyquist):**

$$f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \cdot 3.5 = 7 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Frame time** (one complete set of 8 samples):

$$T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{7000} \approx 142.86 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3 — Effective payload time** (90% of frame):

$$T_{payload} = 0.9 \cdot T_f = 0.9 \cdot 142.86 \approx 128.57 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 4 — Slot width:**

$$\tau = \frac{T_{payload}}{N} = \frac{128.57}{8} \approx 16.07 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 5 — Verify clock allows this:**

$$\tau_{min} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{100{,}000} = 10 \text{ μs}$$

Since $\tau = 16.07 \text{ μs} > 10 \text{ μs}$ ✓ — the clock can accommodate this design.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: PAM-TDM Output Spectrum (Conceptual)

A flat-top PAM-TDM signal $s_{PAM}(t)$ can be modeled as:

$$s_{PAM}(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i(k T_s)\, p(t - k T_s - i\tau)$$

where $p(t)$ is the rectangular pulse shape of width $\tau$. The Fourier transform yields:
- A **baseband spectrum** $M_i(f)$ scaled by the duty cycle $(\tau / T_s)$.
- **Replication spectra** at multiples of $f_s = 1/T_s$ — these are removed by per-channel LPFs at the receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This spectral structure is why a **low-pass reconstruction filter** at each receiver output is mandatory — it removes the spectral replicas centered at $\pm f_s, \pm 2 f_s, \ldots$ and recovers $m_i(t)$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — FDM vs TDM Trade-offs

| Parameter | FDM | TDM |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Noise Susceptibility | High (analog) | Low (digital/PCM) |
| Hardware Complexity | Filters, oscillators | Switches, clocks |
| Synchronization Need | Not strict | **Strict** (frame sync) |
| Channel Addition | Add a subcarrier pair | Add a slot — easy in PCM |
| Crosstalk Source | Spectral overlap | Time jitter, clock drift |
| Best For | Continuous analog | Discrete digital |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FDM Telemetry — Transmitter Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[SENSOR 1\nm1 of t] --> F1[LOW PASS FILTER\nfm cutoff]
    S2[SENSOR 2\nm2 of t] --> F2[LOW PASS FILTER\nfm cutoff]
    S3[SENSOR 3\nmN of t] --> F3[LOW PASS FILTER\nfm cutoff]
    F1 --> M1[SUB MOD 1\nfc1 carrier]
    F2 --> M2[SUB MOD 2\nfc2 carrier]
    F3 --> M3[SUB MOD N\nfcN carrier]
    M1 --> AD[LINEAR ADDER\nCOMPOSITE FDM]
    M2 --> AD
    M3 --> AD
    AD --> RF[RF MODULATOR\nfRF carrier]
    RF --> TX[TX ANTENNA\nCHANNEL]
```

## 4.2 FDM Telemetry — Receiver Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RX[RX ANTENNA] --> RFD[RF DEMOD]
    RFD --> AMP[LINEAR AMP\nLINEAR COMPOSITE]
    AMP --> BPF1[BPF1\nfc1 center]
    AMP --> BPF2[BPF2\nfc2 center]
    AMP --> BPFN[BPFN\nfcN center]
    BPF1 --> DM1[SUB DEMOD 1]
    BPF2 --> DM2[SUB DEMOD 2]
    BPFN --> DMN[SUB DEMOD N]
    DM1 --> OUT1[OUTPUT 1\nm1 hat]
    DM2 --> OUT2[OUTPUT 2\nm2 hat]
    DMN --> OUTN[OUTPUT N\nmN hat]
```

## 4.3 TDM-PAM Transmitter Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CH 1] --> L1[LPF fm]
    A2[CH 2] --> L2[LPF fm]
    AN[CH N] --> LN[LPF fm]
    L1 --> COMM[ELECTRONIC\nCOMMUTATOR\nMUX]
    L2 --> COMM
    LN --> COMM
    CLK[MASTER CLOCK\nfs] --> COMM
    COMM --> PAM[PAM OUTPUT\nSAMPLER]
    PAM --> TX[RF MODULATOR\nTX BLOCK]
```

## 4.4 TDM-PAM Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RX[RX RF DEMOD] --> SYNC[SYNC EXTRACTOR\nBIT AND FRAME]
    SYNC --> CLK[CLOCK RECOVERY]
    RX --> DECOMM[DE COMMUTATOR\nDEMUX]
    CLK --> DECOMM
    DECOMM --> LF1[LPF 1\nreconstruct]
    DECOMM --> LF2[LPF 2\nreconstruct]
    DECOMM --> LFN[LPF N\nreconstruct]
    LF1 --> O1[OUT 1]
    LF2 --> O2[OUT 2]
    LFN --> ON[OUT N]
```

## 4.5 TDM Frame Structure (Time-Domain View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["FRAME PERIOD Tf"] --> S1[SLOT 1\nCH 1 sample]
    F --> S2[SLOT 2\nCH 2 sample]
    F --> SDOTS["..."]
    F --> SN[SLOT N\nCH N sample]
    F --> SG[GUARD\nSYNC PULSE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **sync/guard pulse** at the end of each frame is critical: it marks frame boundaries and triggers the de-commutator reset. Losing sync scrambles all channels.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define Frequency Division Multiplexing. List two practical limitations of FDM systems. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**Model Answer:**
FDM is a technique in which multiple independent signals are transmitted simultaneously over a common channel by translating each signal to a unique, non-overlapping frequency band using subcarrier modulation.

**Two practical limitations:**
1. **Crosstalk** due to spectral overlap between adjacent channels from imperfect filter roll-off.
2. **Intermodulation distortion** caused by non-linearity in the summing amplifier, producing sum/difference frequencies that fall *inside* other channels.

*(2 marks for crisp definition, 1 mark for limitations — as per KTU valuation key.)*

---

### **Q2.** What is the role of synchronization in TDM? Name the two types. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**Model Answer:**
Synchronization aligns the receiver's de-commutator with the transmitter's commutator so that each sample is routed to the correct output channel. Without it, channel cross-routing occurs.

**Two types:**
1. **Bit synchronization** — clock recovery for slot-level timing.
2. **Frame synchronization** — alignment of frame start via a dedicated sync pulse/codeword.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a **3-channel FDM telemetry system**. Discuss the role of subcarrier generators, modulators, and bandpass filters. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 5-channel FDM system uses AM-DSB-SC subcarrier modulation. Each input has $f_m = 3$ kHz, and the guard band is $f_g = 1$ kHz. Subcarriers are spaced $10$ kHz apart starting from $f_{c1} = 25$ kHz. Compute the **total transmission bandwidth** and verify whether the highest channel's upper sideband stays below $70$ kHz. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Explanation [7 Marks]:**
*(Draw the block diagram as in Section 4.1.)*
1. Three sensors feed three LPFs (cutoff $f_m$). [1 mark]
2. Each filtered signal modulates a distinct subcarrier $f_{c1}, f_{c2}, f_{c3}$ using AM modulators. Subcarrier generators provide stable sinusoids. [2 marks]
3. The three modulated subcarrier outputs are linearly summed in a **linear adder** to produce the composite FDM signal. [1 mark]
4. The composite signal modulates an RF carrier $f_{rf}$ for transmission. [1 mark]
5. **Receiver:** RF demod → 3 BPFs tuned to $f_{c1}, f_{c2}, f_{c3}$ → 3 subcarrier demodulators → 3 reconstructed outputs. [2 marks]

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1:** $N = 5$, $f_m = 3$ kHz, $f_g = 1$ kHz, DSB-SC.

**Step 2:** Practical DSB FDM bandwidth:
$$B = N \cdot 2 f_m + (N-1) f_g$$
$$B = 5 \cdot 6 + 4 \cdot 1 = 30 + 4 = 34 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3:** Subcarrier frequencies:
$f_{c1} = 25$ kHz, $f_{c2} = 35$ kHz, $f_{c3} = 45$ kHz, $f_{c4} = 55$ kHz, $f_{c5} = 65$ kHz.

**Step 4:** Highest channel (CH5) upper sideband edge:
$$f_{USB,max} = f_{c5} + f_m = 65 + 3 = 68 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 5:** Since $68$ kHz $< 70$ kHz ✓ — design is valid. The total transmission bandwidth spans from $f_{c1} - f_m = 22$ kHz to $68$ kHz = $46$ kHz. (Cross-check: $34$ kHz assumes contiguous packing; with first channel starting at $22$ kHz and ending at $28$ kHz, then guard, then next, the practical span is wider — full diagram analysis.) **[1 mark]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**(a)** Explain the **TDM-PAM telemetry system** with a block diagram. Discuss how **synchronization** and **commutation** are achieved. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A TDM system handles **6 channels**, each bandlimited to $f_m = 4$ kHz. The clock frequency is **80 kHz**, and **15% of each frame is reserved for guard/sync**. Determine: **(i)** the minimum sampling rate, **(ii)** frame time, **(iii)** slot width, and **(iv)** whether the design satisfies the Nyquist criterion. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — TDM-PAM Explanation [7 Marks]:**
*(Refer to block diagram in Section 4.3.)*
1. Six sensors → six LPFs ($f_m$ cutoff) → fed to a 6:1 electronic commutator (MUX). [1 mark]
2. Master clock drives the commutator sequentially at $f_s$. Each channel is sampled once per frame. [1 mark]
3. Output is a PAM waveform — pulses with amplitudes proportional to instantaneous samples. [1 mark]
4. The PAM signal modulates an RF carrier for radio transmission. [1 mark]
5. **Synchronization:** A sync pulse is inserted at the end of each frame. Receiver extracts it via a sync detector → drives the de-commutator. Both bit sync and frame sync are recovered. [2 marks]
6. **Commutation:** Done electronically (MOSFET switches or multiplexer ICs) — much faster and more reliable than mechanical commutators. [1 mark]

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist):**
$$f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \cdot 4 = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Frame time:**
$$T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3 — Effective payload time (85% of frame):**
$$T_{payload} = 0.85 \cdot 125 = 106.25 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 4 — Slot width (6 equal slots):**
$$\tau = \frac{106.25}{6} \approx 17.71 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 5 — Verify clock allows this:**
$$\tau_{min} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{80{,}000} = 12.5 \text{ μs}$$

Since $\tau = 17.71 \text{ μs} > \tau_{min} = 12.5 \text{ μs}$ ✓ — clock supports this design.

**Step 6 — Nyquist verification:**
Frame rate $f_f = f_s / N = 8000/6 \approx 1333$ Hz. Since each input is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz $> 2 f_m = 8$ kHz ✓ (exactly at the boundary). Recommend $f_s > 8$ kHz (e.g., 9 kHz) for a safety margin. **[1 mark]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **FDM bandwidth formula mismatch:** Many students confuse $B = N f_m$ with $B = N \cdot 2 f_m$. **For DSB-SC**, remember each channel contributes *both* USB and LSB → factor of **2**. For SSB, the factor is **1**. Marks lost: **2 marks per instance**.
> 2. **Forgetting guard band:** KTU problems almost always include a guard band. Omitting $(N-1) f_g$ costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **TDM slot vs frame confusion:** A common mistake is treating $\tau$ (slot) as $T_f$ (frame). Always remember: $T_f = N \tau + T_g$.
> 4. **Synchronization skipped:** In TDM questions, examiners expect explicit mention of *both* bit and frame sync. Omitting this typically loses **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **No block diagram:** Drawing the block diagram is **mandatory** for 7-mark questions. Skip it → automatic **2-mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Points for KTU 2024 Exam (Module 4 — TDM/FDM):**

- 🔹 **FDM** divides the **frequency axis**; **TDM** divides the **time axis**.
- 🔹 Standard telemetry uses **IRIG subcarrier frequencies** from **400 Hz to 165 kHz**.
- 🔹 Practical FDM bandwidth formula: $\,B = N f_m + (N-1) f_g\,$ (SSB) or $\,B = 2N f_m + (N-1) f_g\,$ (DSB).
- 🔹 **Guard bands** prevent crosstalk; **non-linearity** in adders causes **intermodulation** (unfilterable).
- 🔹 **TDM variants:** PAM, PDM (PWM), PPM, PCM — each trades bandwidth for noise immunity.
- 🔹 Nyquist rate: $\,f_s \geq 2 f_m\,$; Frame time: $\,T_f = 1/f_s\,$; Slot: $\,T_f = N \tau + T_g\,$.
- 🔹 **Synchronization** in TDM is *non-negotiable* — has two forms: **bit** and **frame**.
- 🔹 **Carson's Rule** for FM subcarrier bandwidth: $\,B = 2(\Delta f + f_m)\,$.
- 🔹 **PAM** = amplitude varies; **PDM** = width varies; **PPM** = position varies.
- 🔹 Block diagrams are **mandatory** for 7-mark theory sub-questions.
- 🔹 Always state *practical* bandwidth (with guard band) unless explicitly told otherwise.
- 🔹 FDM is preferred for **analog continuous** data; TDM/PCM for **digital** data.
- 🔹 De-commutation requires **LPF reconstruction filters** per output channel to remove spectral replicas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of TDM and FDM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Comparison of TDM and FDM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Multiplexing?

**Multiplexing** is the technique of combining multiple independent information-bearing signals (from different sources) so that they can be transmitted simultaneously over a **single communication channel** (or transmission medium), thereby optimizing the use of costly bandwidth and infrastructure.

At the receiving end, a corresponding **demultiplexer** separates these signals back into their original forms. Multiplexing is the foundational concept behind modern telecommunication trunk lines, telephone networks, satellite links, and telemetry systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Multiplexing is the backbone of telemetry systems studied under Module 4. The two principal analog/digital-domain techniques are **TDM** (Time Division) and **FDM** (Frequency Division).

## 1.2 Formal Definition of TDM (Time Division Multiplexing)

**Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)** is a multiplexing technique in which several independent baseband signals (analog or digital) are transmitted over a **common channel** by allocating to each signal a **disjoint, sequential time slot** within a recurring cycle called a **frame**. The entire bandwidth of the channel is occupied by **one signal at a time**, but the time slots are switched so rapidly that the user perceives simultaneous transmission.

The technique relies on the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**: if each input signal is band-limited to $f_m$ Hz, then sampling it at $f_s \geq 2f_m$ samples/sec preserves all information.

## 1.3 Formal Definition of FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing)

**Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)** is a multiplexing technique in which several independent baseband signals (typically analog) are transmitted simultaneously over a **common channel** by translating each signal to a **distinct, non-overlapping frequency band** using a separate sub-carrier. The channel bandwidth is therefore divided into **frequency slots** that exist concurrently in time.

A **guard band** (a small unused frequency strip) is inserted between adjacent channels to prevent **crosstalk** caused by imperfect filtering and spectral leakage.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Conference Room" Model

Imagine ten people wanting to share a single microphone connected to one loudspeaker in a hall.

- **FDM Analogy → "Different Languages in One Room":** All ten people speak at the *same time*, but each speaks in a unique language (e.g., English, Hindi, French, Tamil…). A listener who knows only one specific language (a band-pass filter) hears only that person clearly. The other "language bands" coexist in the room but are filtered out. Frequencies correspond to *languages*, and guard bands correspond to *silence between translations* — necessary to keep the languages from blending.

- **TDM Analogy → "Strict Round-Robin Speaker Queue":** All ten people share the *same language*, but the moderator (multiplexer) gives each person the microphone for a strict, fixed time slot — say 1 second each. The cycle repeats every 10 seconds. To a distant observer, the conversation appears continuous. Time slots correspond to *turns*, and the frame corresponds to *one complete round*.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics and Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standardized Terms used in KTU board exams:**
> - **Channel Bandwidth ($B$ or $f_m$):** Maximum frequency content of the baseband signal (Hz).
> - **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$):** Rate at which one signal is sampled (Hz).
> - **Frame:** One complete cycle of all multiplexed channels in TDM.
> - **Guard Band ($B_g$):** Unused frequency gap between adjacent FDM channels.
> - **Cross-talk:** Unwanted coupling of one channel's energy into another.

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Baseband bandwidth | $f_m$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| Sampling rate (Nyquist) | $f_s = 2f_m$ | Samples/sec |
| Frame period (TDM) | $T_f$ | seconds |
| Sub-carrier frequency (FDM) | $f_c$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| Guard band | $B_g$ | Hertz (Hz) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of TDM — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Sampling Stage:** Each of the $N$ input signals (band-limited to $f_m$ Hz) is passed through a low-pass filter and sampled by an electronic switch (a rotating commutator or a CMOS analog switch) at $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
2. **Time-Slot Allocation:** The samples of channel 1 occupy slot 1, channel 2 occupy slot 2, … channel $N$ occupy slot $N$. The complete sequence of $N$ samples constitutes **one TDM frame**.
3. **Frame Transmission:** The composite TDM signal occupies the *full* channel bandwidth for the duration of the frame. There is **no frequency separation**; signals are separated purely in **time**.
4. **Synchronization:** A **frame synchronizer** at the receiver uses a unique synchronization word/bits to align the demultiplexer switch to the correct time slot. Loss of sync collapses the entire system.
5. **Demultiplexing:** The synchronized switch routes each time slot to the corresponding output reconstruction filter (typically a sample-and-hold followed by a low-pass filter).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why TDM Requires Synchronization:** Because channels are time-stacked, a drift of even one microsecond causes slot overlap and "interleaving interference." FDM does not need tight slot timing.

## 2.2 Operational Principle of FDM — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Pre-Filtering:** Each input signal is band-limited by an anti-aliasing LPF.
2. **Modulation Stage:** Each signal modulates its own sub-carrier $f_{c,i}$ using AM, DSB-SC, SSB, or FM. The choice of modulation affects bandwidth and fidelity.
3. **Frequency Translation:** The spectrum of each signal is shifted to its assigned band. The $i$-th channel occupies the band $[f_{c,i} - f_m, f_{c,i} + f_m]$.
4. **Combining:** All modulated signals are linearly added in a **summing amplifier** (the composite signal is the *algebraic sum* of all channel spectra, hence FDM is a linear operation).
5. **Guard Bands:** An unused band $B_g$ separates adjacent channels to permit the receiver's band-pass filters to have non-zero roll-off without leaking neighboring channels into each other.
6. **Demultiplexing:** The receiver uses a bank of **band-pass filters**, each tuned to a unique $f_{c,i}$, followed by coherent demodulators and reconstruction LPFs.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value bars in tables — never use `|`.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_s = 2 f_m$ | $f_m$ = max baseband freq |
| 2 | TDM frame duration (synchronous) | $T_f = \dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{1}{2 f_m}$ | Time for one full cycle |
| 3 | Time-slot duration for $N$ channels | $\tau = \dfrac{T_f}{N} = \dfrac{1}{2 N f_m}$ | Each channel's allotment |
| 4 | Bit duration (B bits per sample) | $T_b = \dfrac{\tau}{B}$ | Digital TDM |
| 5 | Channel data rate in TDM | $R_{ch} = 2 N f_m B$ | bits per second (PCM-TDM) |
| 6 | FDM total bandwidth (no guard) | $B_T = N \cdot 2 f_m$ | SSB assumed; for DSB $\times 2$ |
| 7 | FDM total bandwidth (with guard) | $B_T = N \cdot 2 f_m + (N-1) B_g$ | Practical case |
| 8 | Sub-carrier spacing | $\Delta f = 2 f_m + B_g$ | Center-to-center |
| 9 | SNR after multiplexing (TDM, approx) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{TDM} \approx \left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{ch}$ | No cross-channel noise addition |
| 10 | SNR penalty due to guard band (FDM) | $B_T$ grows, hence noise power grows | Reduces effective $S/N$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TDM applications:** Digital telephone trunk lines (T1 = 1.544 Mbps, E1 = 2.048 Mbps), PCM telemetry in satellites (e.g., ISRO's INSAT), spacecraft command/telemetry, digital audio broadcasting (DAB), and modern 4G/5G time-slot scheduling.
- **FDM applications:** Legacy analog telephone long-distance trunks (L-carrier, N-carrier systems), FM stereo broadcasting (L+R / L–R multiplex), TV broadcasting (video + audio + color sub-carrier), cable TV distribution, and radio astronomy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for KTU:** Module 4 (Telemetry) directly uses **PCM-TDM** for spacecraft data transmission, while FDM is used in **FM-FDM telemetry** and **SCADA** power-line carrier systems. The examiner often tests the comparative merits of each.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — TDM Frame Period and Slot Width

**Given:** $N$ analog input signals, each band-limited to $f_m$ Hz. We use synchronous TDM with PCM, $B$ bits per sample.

**Step 1 — Sampling each channel independently.**  
Per the Nyquist theorem, the sampling rate of each individual channel must be:

$$f_s = 2 f_m \quad \text{(samples per second per channel)}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the TDM frame period.**  
A "frame" contains exactly one sample from every channel. Therefore, the frame rate equals the per-channel sample rate:

$$f_{\text{frame}} = f_s = 2 f_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_f = \frac{1}{2 f_m}$$

**Step 3 — Slice the frame into N time slots.**  
Each slot must hold one sample of duration $1/f_s$. Divided among $N$ channels, the slot width is:

$$\tau = \frac{T_f}{N} = \frac{1}{2 N f_m}$$

**Step 4 — Quantize to $B$ bits per sample (PCM step).**  
Within slot $\tau$, we transmit $B$ bits, so bit duration is:

$$T_b = \frac{\tau}{B} = \frac{1}{2 N f_m B}$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate channel bit rate.**  
Total PCM-TDM bit rate is therefore:

$$R_b = \frac{1}{T_b} = 2 N f_m B \quad \text{bits/second}$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling $N$ (more channels) or doubling $f_m$ (wider input) doubles the required bit rate, hence doubles the required channel bandwidth of the transmission medium. This is the fundamental TDM bandwidth–channel trade-off.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — FDM Total Bandwidth

**Given:** $N$ voice signals, each with baseband $f_m = 4 \text{ kHz}$. Each is DSB-SC modulated onto its own sub-carrier. A guard band $B_g$ is used between adjacent channels.

**Step 1 — DSB-SC bandwidth per channel.**  
For a baseband message of width $f_m$ and a DSB-SC modulator, the output spectrum extends from $f_{c,i} - f_m$ to $f_{c,i} + f_m$, giving a width of $2 f_m$ per channel.

**Step 2 — Sum of $N$ non-overlapping channels.**  
Without guard bands, the minimum total bandwidth is:

$$B_T^{\min} = N \cdot (2 f_m) = 2 N f_m$$

**Step 3 — Insert $(N-1)$ guard bands.**  
Each guard band consumes $B_g$ Hz of spectrum, so:

$$B_T = 2 N f_m + (N-1) B_g$$

**Step 4 — Sub-carrier frequency assignment.**  
Starting at $f_{c,1} = f_m$ (lowest), successive sub-carriers are spaced by $\Delta f$:

$$\Delta f = 2 f_m + B_g$$

$$f_{c,k} = f_m + (k-1) \cdot \Delta f, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check (classic KTU example).**  
Take $N = 12$ voice channels, $f_m = 4$ kHz, $B_g = 0.5$ kHz, DSB-SC.

$$B_T = 2(12)(4) + (12-1)(0.5) = 96 + 5.5 = 101.5 \text{ kHz}$$

The composite signal occupies the spectrum $[f_m, f_m + B_T] = [4, 105.5] \text{ kHz}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Signal-to-Noise Ratio Comparison

**TDM:** The dominant noise is **quantization noise** from the ADC. With $B$ bits/sample, the SQNR is:

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 B + 1.76 \text{ dB (approx)}$$

Quantization noise is confined to the time slot of its own channel, so **inter-channel interference is essentially zero**.

**FDM:** The dominant noise is **thermal/channel noise** integrated across the full composite bandwidth $B_T$. Since FDM linearly adds spectra, the noise in any single output channel is determined by the receiver band-pass filter's bandwidth (≈ $2 f_m$). The ratio of useful bandwidth to total bandwidth is:

$$\eta_{BW} = \frac{2 f_m}{2 f_m + B_g} < 1$$

For SSB-FDM this efficiency approaches unity; for DSB-FDM with large $B_g$ it is poor.

**Conclusion:** TDM offers cleaner SNR at the cost of strict timing, while FDM offers circuit simplicity at the cost of bandwidth overhead and potential cross-talk.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| # | Parameter | TDM | FDM |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Domain of separation | Time | Frequency |
| 2 | Signal type supported | Primarily digital (PCM-TDM); analog PAM-TDM also possible | Primarily analog; can carry digital via sub-carrier modems |
| 3 | Bandwidth utilization | Excellent — no guard band in frequency; all bandwidth used | Wastage due to guard bands (especially DSB) |
| 4 | Synchronization | **Mandatory** — frame sync bits required | Not strictly required — only per-channel carrier recovery |
| 5 | Cross-talk susceptibility | Very low (time slots are disjoint) | Higher (filter roll-off, non-linear distortion) |
| 6 | Hardware complexity | Sampling, A/D, sync logic — moderate to high | Modulators, BPF bank, demodulators — high |
| 7 | Noise immunity | High (digital regeneration eliminates noise) | Low (noise accumulates along the analog chain) |
| 8 | Intermodulation distortion | Not applicable (linear in time) | **Critical issue** — non-linear amplifiers cause cross-products |
| 9 | Flexibility / reconfiguration | Easy to add/remove time slots dynamically | Difficult — requires re-tuning of filters and carriers |
| 10 | Propagation delay | Small and uniform (one frame period) | Group delay varies with filter type (Bessel, Butterworth, etc.) |
| 11 | Typical applications | T1/E1 trunks, satellite telemetry, GSM, LTE | FM radio, TV broadcast, cable TV, telephone L-carrier |
| 12 | Cost trend | Decreasing (digital ICs cheap) | Stable (precision analog components) |
| 13 | Power efficiency | Better (digital) | Lower (linear RF amplifiers required) |
| 14 | Suitability for telemetry | **High** — used in PCM-TDM spacecraft links | Moderate — used in FM-FDM low-rate industrial telemetry |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Time Division Multiplexing (TDM). Mention any two advantages of TDM over FDM.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) is a technique in which multiple signals share a common transmission channel by transmitting samples of each signal in **sequential, non-overlapping time slots** within a recurring frame. The channel's full bandwidth is utilized by only one signal at a time, but the time-slot switching happens rapidly enough to appear simultaneous to the user.

**Two advantages of TDM over FDM:**
1. **Better bandwidth utilization** — TDM does not require guard bands between channels, since separation is in time, not frequency. FDM wastes spectrum in guard bands.
2. **Higher noise immunity** — TDM uses digital (PCM) representation, allowing regeneration of the signal at repeaters without noise accumulation, whereas FDM suffers from analog noise build-up.
3. *(Any one of the above may be cited; the third acceptable point is "no intermodulation distortion," as TDM is a linear time-domain process.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a guard band in FDM? Why is it required?**

**Model Answer:**

A **guard band** is a small, unused portion of the frequency spectrum intentionally left vacant between two adjacent FDM channels.

**Why required:**
- Practical band-pass filters cannot have an infinitely sharp roll-off (a "brick-wall" response).
- Without guard bands, the side-lobe energy of one channel would leak into the neighbouring channel, causing **crosstalk** and **interchannel interference**.
- The guard band gives the BPF a finite transition region, allowing the receiver to isolate each channel cleanly.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, usually split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **transmitter and receiver sections of an FDM system**. Clearly state the role of the **modulators, band-pass filters, and demodulators**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** **Twelve voice signals**, each band-limited to **3.5 kHz**, are multiplexed using **DSB-SC FDM** with a guard band of **1 kHz** between adjacent channels. Calculate:
  - (i) The total bandwidth of the multiplexed signal.
  - (ii) The sub-carrier frequency of the 8th channel, if the first sub-carrier is placed at 4 kHz.
  - (iii) The spectral band occupied by the 12th channel. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation:**

The FDM transmitter comprises $N$ parallel branches, each containing an **anti-aliasing LPF**, a **modulator** (DSB-SC, AM, SSB, or FM), and a **summing amplifier** that linearly combines the modulated outputs. The composite signal then modulates a high-frequency RF carrier for transmission.

The FDM receiver consists of a bank of **band-pass filters**, each tuned to a unique sub-carrier $f_{c,i}$. Each filter extracts the spectrum of the $i$-th channel. The extracted signal is then fed to a **coherent demodulator** (mixer + local oscillator) followed by a **reconstruction LPF**, which recovers the original baseband message.

**Roles:**
- **Modulator (Tx):** Translates the baseband message spectrum to a higher frequency band centered on $f_{c,i}$, enabling non-overlapping spectral placement.
- **Band-pass Filter (Rx):** Selects only the $i$-th channel's spectrum and rejects all others; this is the **frequency-domain demultiplexer**.
- **Demodulator (Rx):** Translates the selected channel back to baseband for reconstruction.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 12$, $f_m = 3.5$ kHz, $B_g = 1$ kHz, DSB-SC, $f_{c,1} = 4$ kHz.

**(i) Total bandwidth:**

$$B_T = 2 N f_m + (N-1) B_g$$

$$B_T = 2 \times 12 \times 3.5 + (12-1) \times 1$$

$$B_T = 84 + 11 = 95 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Sub-carrier spacing:**

$$\Delta f = 2 f_m + B_g = 2(3.5) + 1 = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

$$f_{c,8} = f_{c,1} + (8-1) \cdot \Delta f = 4 + 7 \times 8 = 4 + 56 = 60 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Spectral band of the 12th channel:**

$$f_{c,12} = f_{c,1} + 11 \cdot \Delta f = 4 + 88 = 92 \text{ kHz}$$

The 12th channel occupies:

$$[f_{c,12} - f_m, \ f_{c,12} + f_m] = [92 - 3.5, \ 92 + 3.5] = [88.5, \ 95.5] \text{ kHz}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **PCM-TDM system**. Explain the function of the **commutator, frame synchronizer, and reconstruction filter**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** **Eight analog signals**, each with a maximum frequency of **4 kHz**, are sampled at the **Nyquist rate** and converted to **8-bit PCM** for transmission over a TDM link. Compute:
  - (i) The sampling rate of each channel.
  - (ii) The TDM frame period and slot duration.
  - (iii) The bit rate on the transmission line.
  - (iv) The minimum transmission bandwidth (Nyquist criterion). (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation:**

A PCM-TDM transmitter samples each of the $N$ analog inputs through a high-speed **rotary commutator** (electronic switch), converts each sample to a digital word using an **ADC**, inserts **frame synchronization bits**, and transmits the composite **serial bit stream** over a single channel.

The receiver uses a **frame synchronizer** to detect the alignment word and lock a local clock. A **de-commutator** (synchronized demultiplexer) routes the digital word for each time slot to its dedicated **DAC**, and a **reconstruction low-pass filter** smooths the staircase output to recover the original analog waveform.

**Functions:**
- **Commutator (Tx):** Sequential time-slot switch — samples channel 1, then 2, …, then N at $f_s$ per channel.
- **Frame Synchronizer (Rx):** Detects the unique sync word in every frame, regenerates the de-commutator's switching clock, and prevents slot misalignment.
- **Reconstruction LPF (Rx):** Removes the high-frequency spectral images introduced by the sample-and-hold, leaving only the original baseband.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 8$, $f_m = 4$ kHz, $B = 8$ bits/sample.

**(i) Sampling rate per channel (Nyquist):**

$$f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Frame period and slot duration:**

$$T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \ \mu\text{s}$$

$$\tau = \frac{T_f}{N} = \frac{125 \ \mu\text{s}}{8} = 15.625 \ \mu\text{s}$$

**(iii) Bit rate on the line:**

$$R_b = N \cdot f_s \cdot B = 8 \times 8000 \times 8 = 512{,}000 \text{ bps} = 512 \text{ kbps}$$

**(iv) Minimum transmission bandwidth (Nyquist for binary):**

$$B_{\min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{512}{2} = 256 \text{ kHz}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the guard band** in FDM bandwidth calculation (most common: 4–5 marks lost in part (b)).
> 2. **Confusing DSB and SSB** in FDM — DSB gives $2 f_m$ per channel; SSB gives $f_m$. Wrong choice = wrong bandwidth.
> 3. **Confusing frame period with slot duration** in TDM — frame period is the time for *one complete round*; slot is *one channel's slice*.
> 4. **Omitting synchronization** in TDM diagrams — the frame sync block is mandatory; absence of it is a 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Forgetting the $\times 2$ in Nyquist** for both sampling rate and minimum transmission bandwidth.
> 6. **Mixing up the LPF and BPF roles** in FDM receiver — LPF is for *baseband reconstruction*; BPF is for *channel selection*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TDM separates channels in TIME; FDM separates channels in FREQUENCY.** This single sentence is the heart of every KTU question on this topic.
- TDM relies on the **Nyquist sampling theorem** ($f_s \geq 2 f_m$); FDM relies on **band-pass filtering** with **guard bands**.
- For $N$ channels of bandwidth $f_m$ each:
  - **TDM frame period:** $T_f = 1/(2 f_m)$ (independent of $N$).
  - **TDM slot width:** $\tau = 1/(2 N f_m)$ (inversely proportional to $N$).
  - **FDM total bandwidth (with guard):** $B_T = 2 N f_m + (N-1) B_g$ (DSB-SC).
  - **Sub-carrier spacing in FDM:** $\Delta f = 2 f_m + B_g$.
- **TDM** is the preferred choice in **digital telemetry, PCM trunk lines, and spacecraft communication** (Module 4 core).
- **FDM** is preferred in **analog TV, FM radio, and legacy telephone long-haul**.
- **TDM advantages:** no guard-band waste, no intermodulation, easy digital regeneration, flexible reconfigurability.
- **FDM advantages:** no tight synchronization, simpler receiver for analog signals, well-suited to broadcast.
- **TDM disadvantages:** requires precise sync, needs ADC/DAC, larger propagation delay at high $N$.
- **FDM disadvantages:** guard-band wastage, intermodulation distortion, noise accumulation, inflexible channel reassignment.
- **PCM-TDM bit rate:** $R_b = 2 N f_m B$ bits/sec — memorize this formula; it appears in nearly every numerical question.
- **Minimum transmission bandwidth for binary PCM-TDM (Nyquist):** $B_{\min} = R_b / 2$.
- **For telemetry systems** (Module 4 focus), **PCM-TDM** is overwhelmingly dominant because of robustness, error-detection capability, and digital regeneration.
- Always **draw block diagrams** with named blocks (LPF, Modulator, Commutator, Sync, Demod, BPF) — missing blocks cost 1–2 marks in 7-mark sub-parts.
- Always **state assumptions** (e.g., "DSB-SC modulation," "synchronous TDM," "ideal BPF") before solving numericals — this signals to the examiner that you understand the model.

---

# Module 4 — Introduction to Telemetry Standards

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Telemetry Standards** are a set of formally documented, internationally accepted technical specifications, protocols, formats, and procedures that govern the acquisition, processing, transmission, and reception of measurement data from remote, inaccessible, or mobile sources to a receiving station for monitoring, recording, and analysis.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PEICT634 – Communication System and Telemetry*, telemetry standards ensure **interoperability**, **data integrity**, **time synchronization**, and **range safety** across diverse platforms such as aircraft, missiles, spacecraft, industrial process plants, and biomedical instruments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Telemetry = Tele (remote) + Metry (measurement)**
> Standards = The "language rules" that ensure two different telemetry systems built by two different manufacturers can still "talk" to each other correctly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you board a flight from Kochi to Frankfurt. The pilot speaks one language, the air traffic controller in Mumbai speaks another, and the Frankfurt tower speaks a third. Without a **common aviation standard** (ICAO English, standardized altitude reporting, transponder codes, etc.), chaos would ensue.

Similarly, in telemetry:

- The **sensor** in a satellite generates bits in one format.
- The **ground station** in another country expects bits in a different format.
- Without agreed-upon **standards** (bit rates, frame structures, modulation, error correction), the data is useless.

**Telemetry standards are the "ICAO of remote measurement."**

### 1.3 Why Standards are Needed

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Interoperability** | Equipment from multiple vendors must work together |
| **Data Integrity** | Standardized error detection/correction (e.g., Reed-Solomon, convolutional codes) |
| **Time Synchronization** | Events must be time-stamped with a common clock reference |
| **Range Safety** | Flight termination commands must be unambiguous |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Spectrum allocation and emission masks must be respected |
| **Reproducibility** | Test data recorded today must be readable 30 years later |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Telemetry standards are not just "recommendations" — for defense, aerospace, and nuclear telemetry, they are **legally mandated**. A missile test range cannot accept telemetry data that does not conform to **IRIG 106**.

### 1.4 Major Standards Organizations (The "Standard-Making Bodies")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these acronyms — they appear frequently in KTU examinations.**

1. **IRIG** — *Inter-Range Instrumentation Group* (USA, Range Commanders Council – RCC)
2. **CCSDS** — *Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems* (International, NASA/ESA-led)
3. **RCC** — *Range Commanders Council* (USA, parent body of IRIG)
4. **NASA** — *National Aeronautics and Space Administration* standards (e.g., NASA-STD-7009)
5. **ESA** — *European Space Agency* standards (ECSS series)
6. **IEEE** — *Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers* (e.g., IEEE 802.11 for wireless telemetry)
7. **ISA** — *International Society of Automation* (industrial telemetry, ISA100.11a)
8. **IEC** — *International Electrotechnical Commission* (e.g., IEC 61850 for power system telemetry)
9. **ITU-R** — *International Telecommunication Union – Radiocommunication Sector* (spectrum allocation)
10. **DoD** — *Department of Defense* (USA) — MIL-STD-1553, MIL-STD-810

### 1.5 Classification of Telemetry Standards

Telemetry standards can be broadly classified into:

- **By Domain:** Aerospace, Defense, Industrial, Medical, Automotive
- **By Function:** Data format, Time code, Channel coding, Modulation, Range safety, Network
- **By Originator:** Government (RCC, NASA, DoD), International (CCSDS, ITU), Professional (IEEE, IEC)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IRIG Standard Family — The Backbone of Modern Telemetry

The **Inter-Range Instrumentation Group (IRIG)** standards, governed by the **Range Commanders Council (RCC)**, are the **de-facto global benchmark** for telemetry, especially in aerospace and defense. The flagship document is **IRIG Standard 106 – Telemetry Standards** (commonly called "the Telemetry Bible"), currently in its **2023 revision**.

### 2.2 IRIG Time Codes — Pulse Width Modulated Clock References

IRIG defines standardized **time codes** for synchronizing telemetry data. The most widely used are:

| IRIG Code | Bit Rate (bps) | Frame Length | Pulse Width | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IRIG-A** | 1000 | 1 second | 1 ms / 0.2 ms | High-precision missile/satellite tracking |
| **IRIG-B** | 100 | 1 second | 10 ms / 2 ms | Most common, industrial & test range |
| **IRIG-D | 1 pps only | 1 second | 10 ms | Simple timing reference |
| **IRIG-E** | 100 | 10 seconds | 1 ms / 0.2 ms | Legacy systems |
| **IRIG-G** | 10000 | 0.1 second | 0.1 ms / 0.02 ms | Very high-speed applications |
| **IRIG-H** | 1 pps only | 1 minute | 10 ms | Long-duration timing |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exams:** Focus on **IRIG-B** and **IRIG-A** — these are the most frequently tested.

### 2.3 IRIG-B Time Code Structure (Detailed)

The IRIG-B format is the **industry workhorse**. Each 1-second frame contains:

- **100 bits** total (100 bps rate)
- **30 bits** for Seconds (BCD: 0–59, plus index markers)
- **17 bits** for Minutes (BCD: 0–59, plus index markers)
- **12 bits** for Hours (BCD: 0–23, plus index markers)
- **12 bits** for Day-of-Year (BCD: 1–365, plus index markers)
- **27 bits** for Control Functions (CF) — defined by the user for payload data
- **2 reference markers** (position identifier 'P' and index count bits)

The **pulse width** is either:
- **"On-time" marker** (position identifier): 8 ms high + 2 ms low = **10 ms**
- **Binary '1'**: 5 ms high + 5 ms low
- **Binary '0'**: 2 ms high + 8 ms low

### 2.4 PCM (Pulse Code Modulation) Telemetry Standards

The **IRIG 106 Chapter 4** defines standardized **PCM data formats** for serial digital telemetry. The classic PCM frame structure:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classic PCM IRIG Frame Structure:**
> `[Sync Pattern] → [Word 1] → [Word 2] → ... → [Word N] → [Frame Counter] → [Repeat]`

**Key parameters (KTU must-know):**

| Parameter | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Word Length** | 8, 12, 14, 16 bits | Most common: **16 bits** |
| **Frame Length** | Up to 8192 bits | Fixed or variable |
| **Sync Pattern** | 16-, 24-, 30-bit patterns | e.g., `1110101110010000` (16-bit) and `1111101001101011` (EB90 hex, 16-bit) |
| **Bit Rate** | 10 bps to 20 Mbps | Application-dependent |
| **MSB/LSB order** | Either, must be specified | Convention: MSB first |

### 2.5 Channel Coding Standards

Channel coding (error correction) is standardized under **IRIG 106 Chapter 7**:

1. **Convolutional Codes:** Rate 1/2, constraint length 7 (the CCSDS/NASA standard)
   - Generator polynomials: $G_1 = 171_{OCT}$, $G_2 = 133_{OCT}$
2. **Reed-Solomon Codes:** RS(255, 223) — the CCSDS standard for space telemetry
3. **Turbo Codes & LDPC:** Modern additions (LDPC is the CCSDS "near-Earth" standard)
4. **Convolutional concatenated with RS:** Classic deep-space coding (Voyager, Cassini)

### 2.6 Modulation Standards (RF Link)

**IRIG 106 Chapter 2** specifies:

- **FM (Frequency Modulation):** Legacy analog telemetry
- **PM (Phase Modulation):** BPSK, QPSK, OQPSK, GMSK, SOQPSK-TG
- **Tier-1 modulation:** SOQPSK-TG (Shaped Offset QPSK, Telemetry Group) — spectral efficiency of ~2 bps/Hz
- **Tier-2 modulation:** Multi-h CPM (Continuous Phase Modulation)
- **Aeronautical Telemetry Bands:** L-band (1435–1525 MHz, 2200–2290 MHz), S-band, C-band

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the equations you will need for Part B problems.**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| IRIG-B Bit Rate | $R_b = 100 \text{ bps}$ | 1-second frame, 100 bits |
| IRIG-A Bit Rate | $R_b = 1000 \text{ bps}$ | 1-second frame, 1000 bits |
| Frame Rate (PCM) | $R_f = \dfrac{R_b}{N_b \cdot N_w}$ | $N_b$ = bits/word, $N_w$ = words/frame |
| Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Theoretical max data rate |
| Required SNR (BPSK, BER=10⁻⁵) | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \approx 9.6 \text{ dB}$ | With ideal coding |
| Bandwidth (BPSK) | $B_{BPSK} = R_b$ | NRZ line coding |
| Bandwidth (QPSK/SOQPSK) | $B_{QPSK} = \dfrac{R_b}{2}$ | Spectral efficiency |
| Coding Gain | $G_{coding} = \left(\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{uncoded} - \left(\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{coded}$ | In dB |
| Reed-Solomon efficiency | $\eta_{RS} = \dfrac{k}{n} = \dfrac{223}{255} \approx 0.875$ | CCSDS RS |
| Convolutional code rate | $r = \dfrac{k}{n} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ | Standard rate |

> **Note:** Never use the symbol `|` directly inside any markdown table cell; use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value or conditional expressions in formulas.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite Launch (ISRO PSLV):** Uses IRIG-B for timing, CCSDS for space-to-ground link, SOQPSK-TG for telemetry downlink.
- **Defence Missiles (Agni, BrahMos):** Strict adherence to IRIG 106 + MIL-STD-1553.
- **Aircraft Flight Testing:** L-band SOQPSK-TG telemetry as per IRIG 106.
- **Industrial SCADA:** IEC 61850 for substation telemetry, ISA100.11a for wireless process telemetry.
- **Patient Monitoring (ICU):** IEEE 11073 for medical telemetry interoperability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: IRIG-B Frame Capacity Utilization

The IRIG-B format is widely tested. Let us derive the **effective payload capacity** (control function bits) per frame and the **time resolution** it provides.

### 3.1.1 Given Data

> [!NOTE]
> **IRIG-B Specification:** Bit Rate = 100 bps, Frame Period = 1 second.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total bits per frame:**

Since the bit rate is 100 bps and each frame lasts 1 second, the total number of bits per frame is:

$$
N_{total} = R_b \times T_{frame}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
N_{total} = 100 \text{ bps} \times 1 \text{ s} = 100 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

**Step 2 — Bits allocated for time-of-day encoding:**

According to IRIG-B specification:
- Seconds field: 30 bits (including index markers)
- Minutes field: 17 bits (including index markers)
- Hours field: 12 bits (including index markers)
- Day-of-year field: 12 bits (including index markers)

Summing these:

$$
N_{time} = 30 + 17 + 12 + 12
$$

$$
N_{time} = 71 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Position identifier bits and reference markers:**

The frame includes 2 special reference markers (Position Identifiers 'P₀' and 'P₅', plus index counts). Accounting for these structural bits:

$$
N_{ref} = 9 \text{ bits (Position identifiers and index counts)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the user payload (Control Function) bits:**

$$
N_{CF} = N_{total} - N_{time} - N_{ref}
$$

$$
N_{CF} = 100 - 71 - 9
$$

$$
N_{CF} = 20 \text{ bits/frame (low-accuracy CF)}
$$

> In **IRIG-B with extended CF (IEEE 1344 / IRIG-B12x)**, an additional 27 bits are made available by re-allocating index bits, bringing the CF payload to approximately **27 bits per second**.

**Step 5 — Effective payload data rate (CF channel):**

$$
R_{CF} = \dfrac{N_{CF}}{T_{frame}}
$$

$$
R_{CF} = \dfrac{20 \text{ bits}}{1 \text{ s}} = 20 \text{ bps}
$$

(Or up to 27 bps for extended CF mode.)

**Step 6 — Time resolution provided:**

Since the BCD fields give time to the nearest second, and the bit rate is 100 bits/s:

$$
\Delta t_{res} = \dfrac{1}{R_b} = \dfrac{1}{100} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 7 — Comparison with IRIG-A:**

By the same logic, for **IRIG-A** (1000 bps):

$$
\Delta t_{res}^{(A)} = \dfrac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

This 10× improvement in resolution is precisely why IRIG-A is preferred for high-dynamics missile telemetry.

### 3.2 Worked Example: PCM Frame Rate Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A PCM telemetry system uses 16-bit words with 128 words per minor frame and a bit rate of 2 Mbps. Calculate:
(a) The minor frame rate.
(b) The minor frame duration.
(c) The number of minor frames per second.

**Given:**
- $R_b = 2 \text{ Mbps} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$
- $N_b = 16 \text{ bits/word}$
- $N_w = 128 \text{ words/frame}$

**Step 1 — Bits per minor frame:**

$$
N_{bits} = N_b \times N_w = 16 \times 128
$$

$$
N_{bits} = 2048 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

**Step 2 — Frame duration:**

$$
T_f = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{R_b} = \dfrac{2048}{2 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_f = 1.024 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.024 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Frame rate:**

$$
R_f = \dfrac{1}{T_f} = \dfrac{1}{1.024 \text{ ms}} \approx 976.56 \text{ frames/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Major frame (assuming 16 minor frames = 1 major):**

$$
T_{major} = 16 \times T_f = 16 \times 1.024 = 16.384 \text{ ms}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Standard

| Application | Time Code | Channel Code | Modulation | Standard Doc |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Satellite TT&C | IRIG-B + CCSDS | RS(255,223) + Conv | QPSK / SOQPSK | CCSDS 131.0, IRIG 106 |
| Missile Telemetry | IRIG-A | RS + Conv | SOQPSK-TG / FQPSK | RCC IRIG 106 |
| Flight Test | IRIG-B | RS + Conv | SOQPSK-TG | RCC IRIG 106 |
| Industrial Wireless | NTP / PTP | FEC (optional) | IEEE 802.15.4 | ISA100.11a, WirelessHART |
| Power Grid | IEEE 1588 PTP | — | Ethernet/IP | IEC 61850 |
| Biomedical | NTP | — | Wi-Fi/BLE | IEEE 11073 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
