# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define telemetry standards. Mention any two major standards bodies and their primary contribution.**

**Model Answer:**

Telemetry standards are formally documented technical specifications that ensure interoperability, data integrity, time synchronization, and range safety in the transmission of measurement data from remote locations.

**Two major standards bodies:**

1. **IRIG (Inter-Range Instrumentation Group):** Publishes the *IRIG 106 Telemetry Standard*, the global benchmark for PCM data format, time codes (IRIG-A, B, E, G, H), channel coding, and RF modulation in aerospace telemetry.

2. **CCSDS (Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems):** Develops international standards for space telemetry, tracking, and command (TT&C), including packet telemetry, Reed-Solomon coding (RS 255, 223), and proximity-link protocols used by NASA, ESA, ISRO, and other space agencies.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between IRIG-A and IRIG-B time codes.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | IRIG-A | IRIG-B |
|---|---|---|
| Bit rate | 1000 bps | 100 bps |
| Frame period | 1 second | 1 second |
| Bits per frame | 1000 bits | 100 bits |
| Time resolution | 1 ms | 10 ms |
| Pulse width (high) | 1 ms / 0.2 ms | 10 ms / 2 ms |
| Typical application | High-dynamics missile telemetry | General-purpose test ranges & industrial |

> IRIG-A provides 10× better timing resolution but consumes 10× more bandwidth than IRIG-B. For most civilian and industrial applications, IRIG-B is sufficient; IRIG-A is reserved for high-speed missile/satellite tracking.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the IRIG-B time code format with a neat sketch of its pulse train. Mention the function of control function (CF) bits. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, describe the hierarchical organization of telemetry standards (Time reference → Data format → Channel coding → Modulation → Network). Give one example standard from each layer. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) IRIG-B Time Code Format:**

The IRIG-B time code is a **100 bps, 1-second frame** format standardized by the Range Commanders Council (RCC) under document IRIG 200-98. It is widely used for synchronizing telemetry, data acquisition, and recording systems.

**Frame Composition:**

- **Total bits per frame:** 100 bits
- **Time-of-day encoding fields (BCD format):**
  - Seconds: 30 bits (2-digit BCD + index markers)
  - Minutes: 17 bits (2-digit BCD + index markers)
  - Hours: 12 bits (2-digit BCD + index markers)
  - Day of year: 12 bits (2-digit BCD + index markers)
  - Time code total: 71 bits
- **Position identifiers (P₀ and P₅):** 8 ms and 5 ms high pulses used as frame and 0.5-second markers
- **Index count bits:** Subdivide each field (1/10, 1/100 of seconds etc.)
- **Control Function (CF) bits:** ~27 bits reserved for user-defined data (e.g., telemetry status, parity, payload ID, health flags)

**Pulse Width Coding:**

- Position identifier: 8 ms high + 2 ms low
- Binary '1': 5 ms high + 5 ms low
- Binary '0': 2 ms high + 8 ms low

**Function of CF Bits:**
The CF bits allow the same IRIG-B time code wire to carry ancillary digital data alongside the time stamp, such as:
- Leap-second pending indication
- Local time offset (UTC vs local)
- Parity and quality-of-time indicators
- Application-specific status flags

**(b) Hierarchical Telemetry Standards:**

[See Section 4.2 architecture diagram]

The five layers with example standards:

1. **Time Reference:** IRIG-B (1 pulse per second + BCD time)
2. **Data Format:** IRIG 106 Chapter 4 (PCM format) or CCSDS 133.0 (Source packets)
3. **Channel Coding:** CCSDS RS(255, 223) + Convolutional (rate 1/2, k=7)
4. **Modulation:** SOQPSK-TG (Tier-1 per IRIG 106 Chapter 2)
5. **Network Protocol:** iNET (Integrated Network Enhanced Telemetry) or CCSDS Proximity-1

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** What are the major international and national bodies responsible for telemetry standardization? Explain the role of CCSDS in space telemetry with at least two of its recommended standards. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PCM telemetry system operates at a bit rate of 5 Mbps with 12-bit words and 256 words per minor frame. Determine:
(i) The minor frame duration.
(ii) The number of minor frames per second.
(iii) The major frame duration if 8 minor frames form one major frame. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Major Telemetry Standardization Bodies:**

**International Bodies:**
- **CCSDS** (Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems): Space telemetry & command
- **ITU-R** (International Telecommunication Union – Radiocommunication): Spectrum allocation
- **IEC** (International Electrotechnical Commission): Industrial & power-grid telemetry
- **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization): Cross-domain standards

**National Bodies:**
- **RCC / IRIG** (USA): Aerospace & defense telemetry
- **NASA** (USA): Space-specific standards (NASA-STD-7009)
- **ESA** (Europe): European space standards (ECSS series)
- **DoD** (USA): MIL-STD-1553 (avionics databus), MIL-STD-810 (environmental)

**Role of CCSDS in Space Telemetry:**

The CCSDS publishes the famous *Blue Books* which are mandatory for inter-agency space communication. Two key CCSDS standards:

1. **CCSDS 131.0-B — TM Synchronization and Channel Coding:** Specifies Reed-Solomon RS(255, 223), convolutional coding with G₁=171₈ and G₂=133₈, and modern LDPC codes for near-Earth missions.

2. **CCSDS 133.0-B — Space Packet Protocol:** Defines the *Source Packet* structure (6-byte primary header + user data field), enabling efficient multiplexing of multiple data streams (imaging, housekeeping, science) into a single telemetry downlink.

**(b) PCM Frame Calculations:**

**Given:**
- $R_b = 5 \text{ Mbps} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$
- $N_b = 12 \text{ bits/word}$
- $N_w = 256 \text{ words/minor frame}$

**(i) Minor frame duration:**

$$
N_{bits} = N_b \times N_w = 12 \times 256 = 3072 \text{ bits/minor frame}
$$

$$
T_{minor} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{R_b} = \dfrac{3072}{5 \times 10^6} = 6.144 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 614.4 \text{ μs}
$$

**(ii) Number of minor frames per second:**

$$
R_{minor} = \dfrac{1}{T_{minor}} = \dfrac{1}{6.144 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1627.6 \text{ frames/s}
$$

**(iii) Major frame duration (8 minor frames = 1 major):**

$$
T_{major} = 8 \times T_{minor} = 8 \times 614.4 \text{ μs} = 4915.2 \text{ μs} \approx 4.915 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Confusing **Mbps** with **MBps** (factor of 8 error). Always verify bit vs byte.
> 2. **Forgetting sync pattern:** The actual transmitted frame includes a 16- or 24-bit sync word at the start. If the question asks for total transmitted bits, you must add sync overhead to the calculated $N_{bits}$.
> 3. **IRIG-B/CF bits:** Students often answer "20 bits" without mentioning the **extended CF** of 27 bits per IRIG-B12x variant.
> 4. **Not writing units:** Every numerical answer must carry units (μs, ms, bps, fps).
> 5. **Confusing Channel Coding with Source Coding:** Source coding compresses (e.g., JPEG); channel coding adds redundancy for errors (e.g., RS, LDPC). Examiners deduct marks for mixing these up.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- [ ] **Telemetry** = Remote measurement; **Standards** = Agreed-upon rules for interoperability.
- [ ] **IRIG** = Inter-Range Instrumentation Group, governed by **RCC**. Document of record = **IRIG 106** ("Telemetry Bible").
- [ ] **IRIG-B** = 100 bps, 1-sec frame, 10 ms resolution; **IRIG-A** = 1000 bps, 1 ms resolution.
- [ ] **IRIG-B frame = 100 bits total** = 71 bits time + ~20–27 bits CF + reference markers.
- [ ] **PCM frame structure:** Sync word → Data words → Frame counter.
- [ ] **PCM classic word length:** 16 bits (most common), 8/12/14 also used.
- [ ] **CCSDS** = International space-data standard. **Blue Books** = mandatory for inter-agency.
- [ ] **RS(255, 223)** = CCSDS Reed-Solomon standard; efficiency = 223/255 ≈ 0.875.
- [ ] **Convolutional code:** Rate = 1/2, Constraint length = 7, polynomials = 171₈ and 133₈.
- [ ] **SOQPSK-TG** = Tier-1 modulation under IRIG 106; ~2 bps/Hz spectral efficiency.
- [ ] **Aeronautical L-band:** 1435–1525 MHz and 2200–2290 MHz (ITU-R allocation).
- [ ] **MIL-STD-1553** = Avionics multiplex databus (1 Mbps, 16-bit words).
- [ ] **ISA100.11a** = Wireless industrial telemetry standard.
- [ ] **IEEE 11073** = Medical/health-care telemetry interoperability.
- [ ] **CCSDS 131.0** = Channel coding; **CCSDS 133.0** = Space packet protocol.
- [ ] **iNET** = Integrated Network Enhanced Telemetry (modern network-based telemetry replacing point-to-point PCM).
- [ ] **Time resolution formula:** $\Delta t = 1/R_b$.
- [ ] **Frame rate formula:** $R_f = R_b / (N_b \times N_w)$.
- [ ] **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- [ ] Always **label diagrams**, **state assumptions**, and **carry units** in numerical answers.
- [ ] **Know the difference** between source coding (compression) and channel coding (error protection).
- [ ] **Three pillars of standards:** Interoperability + Integrity + Time synchronization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** When an exam question says "explain telemetry standards," structure your answer in this 5-point format: **(i) Definition, (ii) Need for standards, (iii) Major bodies (IRIG, CCSDS, IEEE, ITU), (iv) Key families (time codes, PCM, channel coding, modulation), (v) One real-world example.** This guarantees full marks under the KTU 2024 Scheme valuation key.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Satellite Telemetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Satellite Telemetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Satellite Telemetry** is the specialized branch of remote sensing and telecommunication engineering that deals with the **automatic measurement, encoding, transmission, reception, and decoding of operational, environmental, and scientific data** from a satellite to one or more ground stations using a dedicated Radio Frequency (RF) link. It forms the critical data-return half of the broader **Telemetry, Tracking, and Command (TT&C)** subsystem that governs all unmanned spacecraft.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme context, satellite telemetry is classified under **aerospace telemetry**, distinct from terrestrial telemetry, because it must overcome the unique challenges of the **space-to-ground propagation channel** — including extreme free-space path loss, Doppler shift due to high orbital velocities, ionospheric scintillation, and round-trip light-time delays of up to **~240 ms** for Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO) links.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "ICU Patient Monitor in Orbit":** Imagine a critically ill patient in an isolated intensive-care unit (ICU) who cannot speak. Doctors attach sensors (heart-rate, oxygen, temperature) to the patient. These sensors continuously send readings **wirelessly** to a nursing station several kilometers away, where nurses monitor the displays and can send instructions back. The satellite is the "patient," the on-board sensors are the **transducers**, the wireless link is the **RF downlink**, the nursing station is the **ground station (Earth Station)**, and the doctor's instructions represent the **command (uplink)** path. The entire two-way process is what engineers call **TT&C**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following values are universally accepted in KTU board examinations and must be memorized:

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Earth's equatorial radius:** $R_E = 6378 \text{ km}$
- **Earth's standard gravitational parameter:** $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^2$
- **Standard GEO altitude:** $h_{GEO} \approx 35{,}786 \text{ km}$ above mean sea level
- **Standard LEO altitude range:** $160 \text{ km} \le h_{LEO} \le 2000 \text{ km}$
- **One-way GEO propagation delay:** $\approx 238 \text{ ms}$ (a hard physical limit imposed by $c$)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Satellite Telemetry Cannot Use Terrestrial Methods

Terrestrial telemetry uses cables, optical fiber, or short-range RF (tens of meters to a few kilometers). Satellite telemetry must instead:
1. Survive **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** spanning hundreds of dB.
2. Tolerate a signal that arrives at the ground at roughly **$-150$ dBm to $-160$ dBm** for a typical LEO downlink.
3. Account for the **Doppler shift** because the relative velocity between satellite and ground station can reach $\pm 7.5 \text{ km/s}$ for LEO passes.
4. Survive vacuum, cosmic radiation, and thermal cycling of $-150^\circ\text{C}$ to $+150^\circ\text{C}$.

## 2.2 Architectural Building Blocks

A satellite telemetry chain conceptually contains five functional blocks:

1. **Sensor / Transducer Array** — measures physical quantities (temperature, pressure, current, attitude, payload data).
2. **Signal Conditioning & Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — amplifies, filters, and digitizes analog sensor outputs.
3. **Encoder & Multiplexer (MUX)** — packages multiple data streams (often using **Pulse Code Modulation – PCM**) into a single binary frame.
4. **Modulator & RF Up-converter (Baseband → Carrier)** — shifts the baseband PCM signal onto an RF carrier (e.g., **S-band 2.2 GHz** or **X-band 8.4 GHz**).
5. **High-Power Amplifier (HPA) & Antenna** — boosts the RF signal and radiates it toward Earth.

The ground station reverses this chain using a **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA)**, **down-converter**, **demodulator**, and **de-multiplexer**.

## 2.3 Classification of Satellite Orbits (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners frequently ask to justify orbit choice for a given telemetry application. Memorize the table below.**

| Orbit Class | Altitude (km) | Orbital Period | Key Telemetry Use-Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LEO (Low Earth Orbit) | $160 - 2000$ | $\approx 90 \text{ min}$ | Earth observation, ISS, Starlink |
| MEO (Medium Earth Orbit) | $2000 - 35{,}786$ | $2 - 24 \text{ hrs}$ | GPS, GLONASS, Galileo |
| GEO (Geostationary) | $\approx 35{,}786$ | $24 \text{ hrs}$ | Weather (INSAT), Direct-broadcast TV |
| HEO (Highly Elliptical) | Apogee $\gt 35{,}786$ | $12 \text{ hrs}$ (Molniya) | High-latitude coverage |
| Polar / Sun-synchronous | $\approx 600 - 800$ | $\approx 100 \text{ min}$ | Remote-sensing (Resourcesat) |

## 2.4 Frequency Allocations for Satellite Telemetry

| Band | Frequency Range | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| UHF | $300 - 1000 \text{ MHz}$ | TTC legacy, amateur satellites |
| L-band | $1 - 2 \text{ GHz}$ | GPS, mobile satellite |
| **S-band** | $2 - 4 \text{ GHz}$ | **Primary TT&C band for ISRO satellites** |
| C-band | $4 - 8 \text{ GHz}$ | Weather radar downlink |
| **X-band** | $8 - 12 \text{ GHz}$ | **High-speed payload data downlink** |
| Ku-band | $12 - 18 \text{ GHz}$ | TV broadcast, VSAT |
| Ka-band | $26 - 40 \text{ GHz}$ | New broadband constellations |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every formula below has appeared at least once in KTU board papers. Reproduce this table verbatim in your personal revision notebook.

| Equation | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^{2}$ | Signal power loss over distance $d$ at frequency $f$ |
| FSPL in dB | $FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | Quick numeric estimation |
| Kepler's Third Law | $T^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}}{\mu}\,a^{3}$ | Relates orbital period $T$ to semi-major axis $a$ |
| Orbital Period (circular) | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{a^{3}}{\mu}}$ | Used to compute contact time |
| Link Budget (Rx Power) | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{path} - L_{misc}$ | Received signal power at ground |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{P_r}{kTB}$ | Where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| Doppler Frequency Shift | $\Delta f = f_c \cdot \dfrac{v_{rel}}{c}$ | Frequency offset due to relative motion |
| Slant Range | $d = \sqrt{(R_E+h)^{2} - R_E^{2}\cos^{2}(el)} - R_E\cos(el)$ | True distance between satellite and ground station |
| Orbital Velocity (circular) | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{R_E+h}}$ | Velocity of satellite for Doppler calculation |
| Maximum Doppler (LEO) | $\Delta f_{max} \approx \dfrac{v_{max} \cdot f_c}{c}$ | Worst-case at horizon acquisition |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Disaster Management:** ISRO's INSAT-3DR provides real-time cyclone telemetry.
- **Defence:** Agni, BrahMos flight telemetry uses S/X-band satellite downlinks.
- **Scientific Research:** Chandrayaan-3 (launched 2023) relayed telemetry from the Moon via the Indian Deep Space Network at Byalalu.
- **Commercial IoT:** Modern LEO mega-constellations (Starlink, OneWeb, ISRO's planned GSAT-32 constellation) offer global M2M telemetry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Orbital Period from Newton's Gravitation

**Objective:** Derive $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{a^{3}}{\mu}}$ — the cornerstone of all satellite-orbit telemetry link-duration calculations.

**Step 1 — Equate gravitational force to centripetal force** for a satellite of mass $m$ orbiting Earth (mass $M$) in a circular orbit of radius $a = R_E + h$:

$$\dfrac{GMm}{a^{2}} = \dfrac{m v^{2}}{a}$$

The satellite mass $m$ cancels immediately on both sides, confirming that orbital period is independent of satellite mass.

**Step 2 — Solve for orbital velocity $v$:**

$$v^{2} = \dfrac{GM}{a}$$

The standard gravitational parameter $\mu = GM = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}^{2}$.

**Step 3 — Relate velocity to period** using circumference $= 2\pi a$ and $T = \text{distance}/\text{speed}$:

$$v = \dfrac{2\pi a}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Square both sides and substitute into Step 2:**

$$\left(\dfrac{2\pi a}{T}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{\mu}{a}$$

$$\dfrac{4\pi^{2} a^{2}}{T^{2}} = \dfrac{\mu}{a}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-multiply and isolate $T$:**

$$4\pi^{2} a^{3} = \mu T^{2}$$

$$T^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} a^{3}}{\mu}$$

$$\boxed{T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{a^{3}}{\mu}}}$$

This is **Kepler's Third Law** applied to a circular orbit and is the basis for the famous "90 minutes for LEO" and "24 hours for GEO" facts.

## 3.2 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

**Objective:** Show that signal power falls off as $\dfrac{1}{d^{2}}$ in free space.

**Step 1 — An isotropic radiator distributes its transmitted power $P_t$ uniformly over a sphere of radius $d$.** The Power Flux Density (PFD) at distance $d$ is:

$$PFD = \dfrac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \quad \text{(W/m}^{2}\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — A receive antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ captures power:**

$$P_r = PFD \cdot A_e = \dfrac{P_t \, A_e}{4\pi d^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Use the antenna-aperture relation** $A_e = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$, where $G_r$ is the receive antenna gain and $\lambda$ is wavelength.

**Step 4 — Substitute $A_e$ into the $P_r$ equation:**

$$P_r = P_t \cdot \dfrac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Define the ratio** $\dfrac{P_t}{P_r}$ as the path loss:

$$L_{path} = \dfrac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$

Using $\lambda = c/f$, this becomes the canonical form:

$$\boxed{FSPL = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to decibels** using $10\log_{10}$:

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$$

Substituting $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ and converting $d$ from km and $f$ from MHz yields the familiar engineer's quick-form:

$$\boxed{FSPL_{dB} \approx 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Full Link Budget

A LEO satellite at $h = 600 \text{ km}$ transmits telemetry at $f = 2.2 \text{ GHz}$ (S-band). Given $P_t = 5 \text{ W}$, $G_t = 3 \text{ dBi}$, $G_r = 40 \text{ dBi}$, miscellaneous losses $L_{misc} = 2 \text{ dB}$. Compute the received power $P_r$ at zenith pass.

**Step 1 — Slant range at zenith:** $d = h = 600 \text{ km}$.

**Step 2 — Compute FSPL:**

$$FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(600) + 20\log_{10}(2200)$$

$$FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 55.56 + 66.85 = 154.86 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Convert $P_t$ to dBm:** $P_t = 5 \text{ W} = 5000 \text{ mW}$.

$$P_{t,dBm} = 10\log_{10}(5000) = 36.99 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply link equation:**

$$P_{r,dBm} = P_{t,dBm} + G_t + G_r - FSPL - L_{misc}$$

$$P_{r,dBm} = 36.99 + 3 + 40 - 154.86 - 2$$

$$\boxed{P_{r,dBm} = -76.87 \text{ dBm}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** This received power is well above the threshold of a modern S-band receiver (typically $-140 \text{ dBm}$), confirming a healthy link with a margin of more than **63 dB** — enough to sustain telemetry even at low elevation angles.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Satellite Telemetry System Architecture

The Mermaid flowchart below maps every functional block, from the on-board transducer all the way to the scientist's display terminal at the Mission Control Centre.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Satellite Sensors and Payloads] --> B[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    B --> C[PCM Encoder and Multiplexer]
    C --> D[Baseband Modulator BPSK QPSK]
    D --> E[S Band or X Band Up Converter]
    E --> F[High Power Amplifier HPA]
    F --> G[Satellite Antenna Downlink]
    G --> H[Free Space Propagation Channel]
    H --> I[Ground Station Parabolic Dish Antenna]
    I --> J[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    J --> K[Down Converter and Demodulator]
    K --> L[De Multiplexer and Frame Synchronizer]
    L --> M[Bit Error Rate Analyzer and Data Storage]
    M --> N[Mission Control Center Display]
    N --> O[Command Uplink to Satellite]
    O --> A
```

## 4.2 Telemetry Tracking and Command Subsystem Internal Layout

The TT&C subsystem is a closed-loop architecture. The command uplink keeps the satellite operational, while the telemetry downlink reports its health.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPACECRAFT SEGMENT
        S1[Housekeeping Sensors]
        S2[Payload Sensors]
        S3[Telemetry Encoder]
        S4[Transmitter and TWT A]
    end
    subgraph GROUND SEGMENT
        G1[Earth Station Receiver]
        G2[Telemetry Decoder]
        G3[Mission Analyst Console]
        G4[Command Generator]
        G5[Uplink Transmitter]
    end
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> G5
    G5 --> S4
```

## 4.3 Orbit Selection Decision Tree for Telemetry Missions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need continuous real time coverage} -->|Yes| GEO[Use GEO Orbit]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need high resolution imagery}
    Q2 -->|Yes| LEO[Use LEO 500 to 800 km]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Cover high latitude polar regions}
    Q3 -->|Yes| HEO[Use HEO Molniya or Tundra]
    Q3 -->|No| MEO[Use MEO 20000 km GPS Class]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define satellite telemetry. List any two challenges unique to space-to-ground telemetry links compared to terrestrial telemetry.**

**Model Answer:**
Satellite telemetry is the process of automatically measuring, encoding, and transmitting data from an onboard spacecraft to one or more Earth stations via an RF link, as part of the TT&C subsystem.

Two unique challenges:
1. **Extreme Free-Space Path Loss** — at GEO distances (≈36,000 km) in S-band, FSPL exceeds **190 dB**, demanding high-gain antennas and HPAs.
2. **Doppler Shift** — LEO satellites moving at ~7.5 km/s cause frequency offsets up to ±40 kHz at S-band, which the ground receiver must track.

*(Marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for two valid challenges = 3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Why is the S-band preferred for TT&C in most ISRO satellites, while the X-band is reserved for payload data downlink?**

**Model Answer:**
S-band (2–4 GHz) is preferred for TT&C because it offers a **favourable trade-off between atmospheric attenuation, antenna size, and component maturity**. Rain attenuation at S-band is negligible, and 2.2 GHz antennas are physically small enough to mount on small satellites while still providing adequate gain. X-band (8–12 GHz) is reserved for **high-data-rate payload telemetry** (e.g., imagery from Cartosat) because it offers a much wider usable bandwidth (~500 MHz) and smaller beamwidth for high-resolution ground reception.

*(Marks: 2 for S-band reasoning + 1 for X-band reasoning = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often lose 4–5 marks by writing only the link-budget equation without substituting actual numerical values. The 2024 Scheme explicitly rewards **plug-and-solve** numerical fluency. Always show units, intermediate logarithms, and a clear "Received Power in dBm" final box.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

#### (a) Derive the expression for the orbital period of a satellite in terms of the semi-major axis and Earth's gravitational parameter. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State Newton's law of universal gravitation** for a satellite of mass $m$ orbiting Earth of mass $M$ at radius $a$:

$$F_g = \dfrac{GMm}{a^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Equate to centripetal force** required for circular motion:

$$\dfrac{GMm}{a^{2}} = \dfrac{m v^{2}}{a}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $m$ and solve for $v^{2}$:**

$$v^{2} = \dfrac{GM}{a} = \dfrac{\mu}{a}$$

**Step 4 — Express $v$ in terms of period $T$** using circumference $= 2\pi a$:

$$v = \dfrac{2\pi a}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} a^{2}}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Equate both expressions of $v^{2}$:**

$$\dfrac{4\pi^{2} a^{2}}{T^{2}} = \dfrac{\mu}{a}$$

$$\boxed{T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{a^{3}}{\mu}}}$$

*(Marks: 2 for setting forces + 2 for velocity relation + 2 for substitution + 1 for final boxed equation = 7 marks)*

#### (b) A satellite orbits in a circular LEO at an altitude of 700 km. Compute (i) the orbital period, (ii) the orbital velocity, and (iii) the maximum Doppler shift at S-band 2.2 GHz during a zenith pass. (7 Marks)

**Given:** $h = 700 \text{ km}$, so $a = R_E + h = 6378 + 700 = 7078 \text{ km} = 7.078 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}$. $f = 2.2 \text{ GHz} = 2.2 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz}$. $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}^{2}$.

**(i) Orbital Period:**

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{(7.078 \times 10^{6})^{3}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{3.546 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{8.896 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \times 943.2 \approx 5927 \text{ s} \approx 98.8 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Orbital Velocity:**

$$v = \dfrac{2\pi a}{T} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 7.078 \times 10^{6}}{5927} \approx 7503 \text{ m/s} \approx 7.5 \text{ km/s}$$

**(iii) Maximum Doppler Shift** at zenith (relative velocity = full orbital velocity):

$$\Delta f_{max} = \dfrac{v \cdot f}{c} = \dfrac{7503 \times 2.2 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta f_{max} \approx 55{,}022 \text{ Hz} \approx 55 \text{ kHz}}$$

*(Marks: 2 each for (i), (ii), (iii) with correct numerical substitution = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a satellite telemetry system. Distinguish clearly between the space segment and the ground segment. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer Outline:**

The architecture comprises two segments linked by an RF propagation channel:

**Space Segment** (functions in the order data flows):
1. Sensors (analog transducers measuring temperature, voltage, current, attitude).
2. Signal conditioning + ADC.
3. PCM encoder + time-division multiplexer (TDM).
4. Modulator (typically BPSK or QPSK for telemetry).
5. Up-converter to S/X-band carrier.
6. HPA (Travelling Wave Tube Amplifier / Solid-State Power Amplifier).
7. Downlink antenna.

**Ground Segment** (reverse chain):
1. Tracking parabolic dish with auto-tracking.
2. LNA + down-converter.
3. Demodulator + bit synchronizer.
4. Frame de-multiplexer + decoder.
5. Real-time display + archival storage.

**Distinction Table (must appear in answer):**

| Parameter | Space Segment | Ground Segment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Power Source | Solar panels + battery | Grid power + UPS |
| Maintenance | None (post-launch) | Routine maintenance possible |
| HPA Output | Few watts to ~50 W | High-power uplink (~2 kW) |
| Antenna | Omnidirectional to medium gain | Large parabolic (≥10 m for X-band) |

*(Marks: 3 for block diagram + 2 for space-segment functions + 2 for ground-segment functions = 7 marks)*

#### (b) For a satellite at GEO altitude transmitting at 4 GHz with EIRP = 50 dBW, ground station antenna gain = 50 dBi, and system noise temperature = 300 K, compute (i) the free-space path loss, (ii) the received carrier power, and (iii) the carrier-to-noise spectral density ratio $C/N_0$. (7 Marks)

**Given:** $d = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$, $f = 4 \text{ GHz}$, $EIRP = 50 \text{ dBW}$, $G_r = 50 \text{ dBi}$, $T_{sys} = 300 \text{ K}$, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

**(i) FSPL:**

$$FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(35{,}786) + 20\log_{10}(4000)$$

$$FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 91.07 + 72.04 = 195.56 \text{ dB}$$

**(ii) Received Carrier Power $C$:**

Using $C_{dBW} = EIRP + G_r - FSPL$:

$$C_{dBW} = 50 + 50 - 195.56 = -95.56 \text{ dBW}$$

**(iii) Carrier-to-Noise Spectral Density $C/N_0$:**

$$N_0 = kT_{sys} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 = 4.14 \times 10^{-21} \text{ W/Hz}$$

$$C/N_0 \text{ (linear)} = \dfrac{2.78 \times 10^{-10}}{4.14 \times 10^{-21}} \approx 6.71 \times 10^{10}$$

$$C/N_0 \text{ (dB-Hz)} = 10\log_{10}(6.71 \times 10^{10}) \approx 108.27 \text{ dB-Hz}$$

*(Marks: 2 + 2 + 3 with one mark reserved for correct unit consistency = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Satellite Telemetry Introduction**

- [ ] **TT&C** stands for **Telemetry, Tracking, and Command** — telemetry is the "downlink data" half only.
- [ ] **GEO altitude = 35,786 km**, **LEO = 160–2000 km**, **MEO = 2000–35,786 km**.
- [ ] Memorize $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}^{2}$ and $R_E = 6378 \text{ km}$.
- [ ] The two-equation foundation: $T = 2\pi\sqrt{a^{3}/\mu}$ and $FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log d_{km} + 20\log f_{MHz}$.
- [ ] **S-band (2.2 GHz)** for TT&C, **X-band (8.4 GHz)** for payload data — write this in every orbit-selection answer.
- [ ] **Maximum Doppler for LEO at S-band ≈ 40–55 kHz** — the receiver must use AFC/PLL.
- [ ] **GEO round-trip delay ≈ 0.5 s** — affects voice and real-time command loops.
- [ ] **PCM + BPSK/QPSK** is the canonical modulation stack for satellite telemetry.
- [ ] Always end a link-budget numerical with a clearly **boxed** answer in **dBm** or **dBW**.
- [ ] Mention **Free-Space Path Loss**, **Doppler**, **propagation delay**, and **ionospheric scintillation** whenever asked about "challenges" — these four are the examiner's holy grail.
- [ ] ISRO examples examiners love: **INSAT, IRS, Chandrayaan, GSAT, Mangalyaan**.
- [ ] For 14-mark derivations, always show **force balance → velocity → period substitution**, never skip steps.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Telemetry and Telecontrol Schemes Related to Industry and Space Exploration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Typical Telemetry and Telecontrol Schemes Related to Industry and Space Exploration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Telemetry** is the process of automatic measurement and wireless transmission of data from remote or inaccessible sources to a receiving station for monitoring, recording, and analysis. **Telecontrol** (also termed *telecommand* or *supervisory control*) is the complementary process of transmitting control signals from a central station to remote actuators, machines, or spacecraft subsystems to alter their state or function.

In the context of **Module 4** of the KTU PEICT634 syllabus, the term *"typical schemes"* refers to the standardized, architecturally complete end-to-end communication systems that integrate sensors, transducers, encoders, transmitters, the propagation medium, the receiver, the data processing unit, and the command/control uplink — all configured for a specific real-world deployment context.

The two principal application domains emphasized in this module are:

1. **Industrial Telemetry & Telecontrol** — fixed-site or plant-wide monitoring of process variables (temperature, pressure, flow, level, voltage, current) using wired or RF links, with supervisory control via SCADA/DCS frameworks.
2. **Space Exploration Telemetry & Telecontrol** — bidirectional RF communication between ground stations and spacecraft (satellites, probes, manned vehicles), including telecommand uplink, telemetry downlink, tracking, and ranging.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Hospital Patient Monitor"**
> Think of a critically ill patient in an ICU. The patient is connected to sensors that continuously measure heart rate, blood oxygen, and blood pressure. These measurements are *telemetered* via cables to a nursing station monitor and a doctor's smartphone. Simultaneously, the doctor can remotely *telecontrol* an infusion pump to administer medicine. This is **closed-loop biomedical telemetry**.
> Now scale this analogy:
> - In an **oil refinery**, the "patient" is a pressure vessel 5 km away, and the "doctor" is the control room operator.
> - In a **Mars rover**, the "patient" is a robot on another planet, and the "doctor" is a scientist at NASA's Deep Space Network — the "nurse's cable" is replaced by a 22-minute-light-delay radio link.

The essential idea is the same: **measure remotely, decide remotely, act remotely, repeat**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

The following constants and standards are universally referenced in KTU board exam questions for this topic:

| Symbol / Standard | Value / Definition | Context of Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed of light, c** | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | RF link delay calculations |
| **Free-space path loss, FSPL** | **(4πd/λ)²** | Uplink/downlink budget |
| **S-band** | **2–4 GHz** | Near-Earth spacecraft telemetry (e.g., ISRO) |
| **X-band** | **8–12 GHz** | Deep-space telemetry (e.g., NASA DSN) |
| **Ka-band** | **26.5–40 GHz** | High-bandwidth planetary missions |
| **IRIG (Inter-Range Instrumentation Group)** | **Telemetry standards (106, 108, 118, etc.)** | Industrial & aerospace TM standards |
| **SCADA** | Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition | Industrial telecontrol architecture |
| **CCSDS** | Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems | Space data link protocol |
| **PCM** | Pulse Code Modulation | Standard telemetry encoding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Examiners *frequently* ask students to identify which frequency band is used for a given space mission. Memorize the band-to-mission mapping: **S-band → LEO/ISRO satellites**, **X-band → deep space (Voyager, MRO)**, **Ka-band → high-rate modern missions (TGO, Juno)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Telemetry Schemes

Telemetry systems can be classified along four orthogonal axes that examiners love to test:

### 2.1.1 By Transmission Channel
- **Wire-line / Wired telemetry** — uses copper pairs, coaxial, or fiber (typical of industrial plants).
- **Wireless / Radio telemetry** — uses RF or optical carriers (typical of remote or mobile platforms like spacecraft).

### 2.1.2 By Signal Nature
- **Analog telemetry** — direct proportional representation of measurand as a voltage/current.
- **Digital telemetry** — measurand is sampled, quantized, and encoded into binary words (predominant in modern systems).

### 2.1.3 By Multiplexing Strategy
- **FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing)** — multiple subcarrier tones, classic in older industrial plants.
- **TDM (Time Division Multiplexing)** — sequential sampling, dominant in PCM-based systems.
- **CDM (Code Division Multiplexing)** — spread-spectrum, used in GPS and some telemetry links.

### 2.1.4 By Directionality
- **Simplex telemetry** — one-way measurement (most older plant links).
- **Half-duplex** — measurement and command share one channel.
- **Full-duplex** — simultaneous uplink and downlink (standard for spacecraft).

## 2.2 The Closed-Loop Telecontrol Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five Canonical Blocks** (must be drawn in any KTU block-diagram question):
> 1. **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** — sensor + signal conditioner + multiplexer + ADC.
> 2. **Encoder / Frame Formatter** — PCM encoder, encryptor, convolutional coder.
> 3. **Transmitter (Tx) / Modulator** — BPSK, QPSK, or GMSK modulator + RF upconverter + HPA.
> 4. **Channel / Link** — wired pair, optical fiber, or free-space RF path.
> 5. **Receiver (Rx) / Demodulator** — LNA + downconverter + demod + decoder + de-framer.
> 6. **Command Uplink (for telecontrol)** — inverse path: encoder → Tx → link → Rx → decoder → actuator.

## 2.3 Industrial Telemetry — Scheme Examples

### 2.3.1 Oil & Gas Pipeline Telemetry
A typical cross-country pipeline (e.g., 1000 km of crude oil line) requires:
- **Pressure transducers** at pumping stations (every 50–80 km).
- **Flow meters** (Coriolis or ultrasonic) at receipt/delivery points.
- **Temperature sensors** for product heating.
- **Cathodic protection** voltage monitoring to detect pipeline corrosion.
- **Leak detection** via negative-pressure-wave or mass-balance methods.

Data is telemetered via:
- **Primary link:** Licensed UHF radio (450–470 MHz) with repeater towers.
- **Backup link:** Satellite (VSAT — Very Small Aperture Terminal) in Ku-band.
- **Tertiary link:** Cellular 4G/5G or fiber along the right-of-way.

The control room (master station) issues telecontrol commands to:
- **Motorized valves** (open/close/throttle).
- **Pump start/stop** and speed setpoints (VFD control).
- **Pressure relief** valves and emergency shutdown (ESD) systems.

### 2.3.2 Power System Telemetry
A power-grid substation uses:
- **Current Transformers (CTs)** and **Potential Transformers (PTs)** for line parameters.
- **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)** for synchrophasor data at 30–120 samples/sec.
- **SCADA RTU (Remote Terminal Unit)** at the substation.
- **IEC 61850** protocol over fiber/Ethernet for communication to the Energy Management System (EMS).

### 2.3.3 Manufacturing / Factory Telemetry
A modern *Industry 4.0* plant uses:
- **IoT sensors** on every machine.
- **WirelessHART or ISA100.11a** wireless fieldbus.
- **MQTT/OPC-UA** protocols over Ethernet/Wi-Fi/5G.
- **Cloud-based SCADA** dashboards.

## 2.4 Space Exploration Telemetry — Scheme Examples

### 2.4.1 LEO Satellite Telemetry (e.g., ISRO's IRS series)
- **Downlink:** S-band (2.2 GHz) PCM/PSK/PM modulation.
- **Uplink telecommand:** S-band or VHF (VHF for older satellites).
- **Ground station:** ISTRAC (ISRO Telemetry, Tracking and Command Network) with stations at Bangalore, Lucknow, Sriharikota, etc.
- **Data rate:** Typically 1–10 Mbps for Earth-observation satellites.
- **Coding:** Convolutional coding (rate 1/2, K=7) with Viterbi decoding; Reed-Solomon outer code.

### 2.4.2 Deep Space Network (NASA DSN)
- **Sites:** Goldstone (USA), Canberra (Australia), Madrid (Spain) — 120° apart for continuous coverage.
- **Antennas:** 34-m and 70-m parabolic dishes.
- **Bands:** S (2 GHz), X (8 GHz), Ka (32 GHz), and recently optical (laser) for LCRD.
- **Coding:** Turbo codes, LDPC codes (consultative CCSDS standards).
- **Range & Doppler tracking:** Two-way coherent link for precise spacecraft position determination.

### 2.4.3 Manned Spaceflight (e.g., Gaganyaan, ISS)
- **S-band** for voice and low-rate telemetry.
- **Ku-band** for high-rate video downlink.
- **TDRS (Tracking and Data Relay Satellite)** constellation provides near-continuous coverage.
- **Multiple redundancy** in transponders (command, telemetry, ranging) — failure of one does not lose the mission.

### 2.4.4 Interplanetary Probes (e.g., Mangalyaan, Voyager)
- **Mangalyaan (MOM):** S-band uplink/downlink with occasional X-band.
- **Voyager 1/2 (now in interstellar space):** S-band (backup) and X-band; signal strength at Earth is ~10⁻²² W/m², requiring the 70-m DSN antennas and cryogenically cooled maser amplifiers.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically standard, derivable, and syllabus-aligned** for the KTU 2024 PEICT634 Module 4 examination.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free-Space Path Loss** | $\text{FSPL} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ or in dB: $\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$ | dB | RF link budget (industrial & space) |
| 2 | **Propagation delay** | $\tau = d / c$ | seconds | Command/telemetry latency |
| 3 | **One-way light time (1 AU)** | $t \approx 500 \text{ s} = 8.33 \text{ min}$ | seconds | Earth ↔ Sun distance |
| 4 | **Earth ↔ Moon one-way delay** | $t \approx 1.3 \text{ s}$ | seconds | Lunar mission design |
| 5 | **Earth ↔ Mars one-way delay** | $t \approx 4$ to $24 \text{ min}$ (varies with orbital position) | minutes | Mars mission telecontrol |
| 6 | **Shannon–Hartley capacity** | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits/sec | Theoretical max telemetry rate |
| 7 | **EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power)** | $\text{EIRP}_{\text{dBW}} = P_{\text{Tx,dBW}} + G_{\text{Tx,dBi}} - L_{\text{feed,dB}}$ | dBW | Spacecraft/ground Tx |
| 8 | **Received power** | $P_{\text{Rx}} = \frac{P_{\text{Tx}} G_{\text{Tx}} G_{\text{Rx}} \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} d^{2} L_{\text{other}}}$ | watts | Link budget |
| 9 | **Carrier-to-Noise ratio** | $\frac{C}{N_{0}} = \text{EIRP} + \frac{G}{T} - k - L_{\text{path}} - L_{\text{atm}}$ | dB-Hz | Space-link budget |
| 10 | **Sampling rate (Nyquist)** | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | Hz | PCM encoding in TM |
| 11 | **Quantization SNR (uniform)** | $\text{SNR}_{q} \approx 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | dB | PCM encoder performance |
| 12 | **Baud rate vs bit rate (M-PSK)** | $R_{b} = R_{s} \log_{2}M$ | bps | Modulation choice |
| 13 | **BER (BPSK, AWGN)** | $P_{e} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_{b}/N_{0}}\right)$ | — | Link margin calculation |
| 14 | **Maximum round-trip for safe control** | Round-trip delay < process time constant | seconds | Telecontrol loop stability |
| 15 | **Sun noise temperature increase** | up to **10,000 K** at low elevation angles | K | DSN Ka-band operations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Note the distinction between $(4\pi d / \lambda)^2$ (linear) and its dB form $32.44 + 20\log d + 20\log f$. Examiners often give a numeric problem (e.g., *"Find the path loss at 4 GHz over 36,000 km GEO distance"*) and expect the dB form for quick computation. Also: **always use units of km and MHz** in the log form — the constant 32.44 assumes so.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipeline monitoring** prevents catastrophic leaks (e.g., the 2010 Kalamazoo River spill was detected by telemetry only 17 hours late — better schemes now achieve detection in <5 minutes).
- **Power grid SCADA** prevents blackouts by enabling automatic load shedding and frequency control.
- **Spacecraft telemetry** allows mission control to know the health of a multi-million-dollar satellite 1 AU away; telecommand is the *only* way to correct anomalies (e.g., the 1999 Mars Climate Orbiter was lost because of a unit mismatch — a telemetry interpretation error).
- **Manned spaceflight** is fundamentally impossible without bidirectional, low-latency, high-reliability telemetry and telecontrol.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss for a GEO Telemetry Link

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A geostationary (GEO) satellite at altitude $h = 35{,}786$ km communicates with an Earth station in the S-band at $f = 2.2$ GHz. Compute (a) the slant range, (b) the free-space path loss, and (c) the one-way propagation delay. Assume the Earth station is on the equator directly beneath the satellite (worst-case elevation = 90°).

### Step 1 — Identify the knowns

- $h = 35{,}786$ km (GEO altitude, a standard KTU-expected constant)
- $f = 2.2$ GHz
- For elevation angle = 90°, the slant range equals the altitude directly.

### Step 2 — Compute the slant range $d$

$$
d = h = 35{,}786 \text{ km} = 3.5786 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the wavelength $\lambda$

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{2.2 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz}} = 0.1364 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the free-space path loss in linear units

$$
\text{FSPL} = \left(\frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda} = \frac{4 \pi (3.5786 \times 10^{7})}{0.1364} = \frac{4.496 \times 10^{8}}{0.1364} = 3.297 \times 10^{9}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{FSPL} = (3.297 \times 10^{9})^{2} = 1.087 \times 10^{19} \quad \text{(linear)}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to dB

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.087 \times 10^{19}) = 190.4 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 6 — Cross-check with the log-form

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(35{,}786) = 20 \times 4.5536 = 91.07 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(2200) = 20 \times 3.3424 = 66.85 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 91.07 + 66.85 = 190.36 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Both methods agree to within rounding (~190.4 dB).** This is the expected FSPL for an S-band GEO link. The path loss for a Ku-band (12 GHz) GEO link is *about 15 dB higher* due to the higher frequency.

### Step 7 — One-way propagation delay

$$
\tau = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{3.5786 \times 10^{7}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 0.1193 \text{ s} \approx 119 \text{ ms}
$$

This delay is negligible for voice, but for two-way ranging it must be precisely known (to microsecond accuracy) for orbit determination.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mars-Earth Round-Trip Delay

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Mars rover is at opposition (closest approach) and at conjunction (farthest). Compute the one-way and round-trip light times.

### Step 1 — Minimum distance (opposition)

Mars-Earth minimum distance $\approx 54.6 \times 10^{6}$ km.

$$
t_{\text{min,one-way}} = \frac{54.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ km}}{3 \times 10^{5} \text{ km/s}} = 182 \text{ s} \approx 3.03 \text{ min}
$$

### Step 2 — Maximum distance (conjunction)

Mars-Earth maximum distance $\approx 401 \times 10^{6}$ km.

$$
t_{\text{max,one-way}} = \frac{401 \times 10^{6}}{3 \times 10^{5}} = 1336.7 \text{ s} \approx 22.3 \text{ min}
$$

### Step 3 — Round-trip times (uplink + downlink)

$$
t_{\text{min,RT}} = 2 \times 3.03 = 6.06 \text{ min}
$$

$$
t_{\text{max,RT}} = 2 \times 22.3 = 44.6 \text{ min}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Because round-trip times exceed **6 minutes at minimum**, no closed-loop real-time telecontrol is possible on Mars. Every command sent is pre-planned and uploaded as a *sequence* (a "command queue"), and every rover action must be autonomously safe (the *autonomy principle*).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Link Budget for a LEO Earth-Observation Downlink

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A LEO satellite at 500 km altitude transmits Earth-observation data at $f = 8.2$ GHz (X-band). The satellite EIRP = 20 dBW. The ground antenna has $G/T = 30$ dB/K. The system requires $\frac{E_b}{N_0} = 9.6$ dB for a BPSK link at BER = $10^{-5}$. Compute the achievable data rate.

### Step 1 — Slant range (assume worst-case grazing, ~ 90° elevation difference small)

For a LEO at 500 km, the maximum slant range (0° elevation) is:

$$
d_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{R_{E}^{2} + (R_{E} + h)^{2} - 2 R_{E}(R_{E} + h)\cos\theta}
$$

But for a pass above the horizon and 0° elevation:

$$
d_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{(R_{E} + h)^{2} - R_{E}^{2}} = \sqrt{2 R_{E} h + h^{2}}
$$

With $R_E = 6371$ km, $h = 500$ km:

$$
d_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{2 \times 6371 \times 500 + 500^{2}} = \sqrt{6.871 \times 10^{6}} = 2621 \text{ km}
$$

### Step 2 — Free-space path loss at 8.2 GHz

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(2621) + 20 \log_{10}(8200)
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(2621) = 68.37 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(8200) = 78.27 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 68.37 + 78.27 = 179.08 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $\frac{C}{N_0}$ at the receiver

$$
\frac{C}{N_{0,\text{dB-Hz}}} = \text{EIRP}_{\text{dBW}} + \left(\frac{G}{T}\right)_{\text{dB/K}} - k_{\text{dB}} - \text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} - L_{\text{atm}}
$$

where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K $\Rightarrow k_{\text{dB}} = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz, and assume $L_{\text{atm}} = 1$ dB.

$$
\frac{C}{N_{0}} = 20 + 30 + 228.6 - 179.08 - 1 = 98.52 \text{ dB-Hz}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to $\frac{E_b}{N_0}$

$$
\frac{E_{b}}{N_{0}} = \frac{C}{N_{0}} - 10 \log_{10}(R_{b})
$$

Setting $\frac{E_b}{N_0} = 9.6$ dB:

$$
9.6 = 98.52 - 10 \log_{10}(R_{b})
$$

$$
10 \log_{10}(R_{b}) = 98.52 - 9.6 = 88.92
$$

$$
R_{b} = 10^{8.892} \approx 78 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The LEO X-band link can support roughly 78 Mbps — sufficient for moderate-resolution Earth-observation imagery. With coding gain (e.g., rate 1/2 convolutional + Viterbi giving ~5 dB coding gain) and a higher-gain ground antenna, rates can be pushed to hundreds of Mbps.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Industrial vs. Space Schemes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is **examination-worthy** — students can be asked to compare and contrast. It crystallizes the design differences between the two application domains.

| Parameter | Industrial Telemetry & Telecontrol | Space Exploration Telemetry & Telecontrol |
|---|---|---|
| **Distance** | Meters to tens of km | 100s of km to **billions of km** (interstellar) |
| **Channel** | Wired, fiber, licensed RF, cellular | Free-space RF, optical (laser) |
| **Latency tolerance** | Milliseconds (closed-loop control) | **Minutes to hours** — mostly store-and-forward or autonomous |
| **Data rate** | kbps to Mbps (process data) | bps (deep space) to Gbps (LEO) |
| **Power budget** | Grid-powered at both ends | **Solar/battery** at spacecraft — extreme constraint |
| **Band** | Sub-GHz ISM, UHF, cellular bands | **S, X, Ka, optical** |
| **Standards** | Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850, OPC-UA | **CCSDS**, IRIG-106, ECSS |
| **Reliability** | 99.9% to 99.99% | **99.9999%+** (with redundancy) — life-critical for manned |
| **Antenna size (Rx)** | Small patch / dipole | **34 m, 70 m parabolic dishes** (DSN) |
| **Coding** | Light (CRC, simple FEC) | **Convolutional, Turbo, LDPC, Reed-Solomon** |
| **Cost** | Thousands of USD per node | **Hundreds of millions USD per mission** |
| **Failure consequence** | Production loss, possible safety incident | **Loss of mission / loss of crew** |
| **Topology** | Star, ring, mesh (PLCs, RTUs) | Point-to-point (spacecraft ↔ ground) + relay (TDRS) |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Quantization SNR for Industrial PCM Telemetry

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A process-control PCM telemetry system uses 12-bit uniform quantization. Compute the theoretical maximum signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR) for a full-scale sinusoidal input.

### Step 1 — Apply the standard formula

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 N + 1.76
$$

With $N = 12$:

$$
\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 2 — Engineering interpretation

- A 12-bit ADC gives roughly **74 dB of dynamic range** — equivalent to about 12 "effective bits" of clean measurement.
- This is sufficient for most industrial process variables (temperature, pressure) where a 0.1% resolution is acceptable.
- For high-precision power metering or vibration analysis, **16-bit or 24-bit** ADCs are used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** The formula above is for a *full-scale sine wave*. For a *random Gaussian* input, the formula changes slightly to $6.02N - 7.27$ dB. Examiners occasionally test this distinction.

## 3.6 Worked Example — Time-Delay Stability Constraint for Telecontrol

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A closed-loop telecontrol system controls the temperature of a chemical reactor. The plant time constant is $T_p = 120$ s. The round-trip communication delay is $\tau = 6$ s. The controller is a simple proportional controller with gain $K$. Determine the maximum $K$ for stability using a first-order Padé approximation of the delay.

### Step 1 — Plant transfer function

$$
G_{p}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + T_{p} s} = \frac{1}{1 + 120 s}
$$

### Step 2 — Communication delay (Padé approximation, 1st order)

$$
e^{-\tau s} \approx \frac{1 - \tau s / 2}{1 + \tau s / 2} = \frac{1 - 3s}{1 + 3s}
$$

### Step 3 — Open-loop transfer function

$$
G_{\text{OL}}(s) = K \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 120 s} \cdot \frac{1 - 3s}{1 + 3s}
$$

### Step 4 — Closed-loop characteristic equation

$$
1 + G_{\text{OL}}(s) = 0
$$

$$
(1 + 120s)(1 + 3s) + K(1 - 3s) = 0
$$

Expand:

$$
1 + 3s + 120s + 360 s^{2} + K - 3Ks = 0
$$

$$
360 s^{2} + (123 - 3K) s + (1 + K) = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Routh–Hurwitz stability criterion

For a second-order polynomial $a s^{2} + b s + c = 0$, stability requires $a, b, c > 0$.

- $a = 360 > 0$ ✓
- $c = 1 + K > 0$ → $K > -1$ (always true for positive gain)
- $b = 123 - 3K > 0$ → $K < 41$

### Step 6 — Conclusion

$$
\boxed{0 < K < 41}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** The communication delay reduces the *maximum allowable controller gain*. In a non-telecontrol scenario (no delay), the gain could be arbitrarily high (limited only by actuator saturation). The delay imposes a hard stability ceiling. This is why **fast industrial loops (motor control, robotics) demand low-latency protocols** like EtherCAT or PROFINET, while slow loops (reactor temperature) can tolerate 6+ seconds of delay.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define telemetry and telecontrol. Give one example of each in (i) a power system substation, and (ii) a Mars rover mission.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Telemetry** is the automatic measurement and transmission of data from a remote point to a central monitoring station. **Telecontrol** is the reverse process — sending control commands from a central station to remote equipment to alter its operation.
- **(i) Power substation:** Telemetry of line voltage, current, and frequency via SCADA RTU. Telecontrol of circuit breaker open/close from the load dispatch center.
- **(ii) Mars rover:** Telemetry of wheel motor currents, battery state-of-charge, and panoramic images sent via X-band. Telecontrol of mobility commands (e.g., "drive forward 5 m, then turn 30°") uploaded as a command sequence.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-4 Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Model Paper 2, Q8]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a typical industrial telemetry and telecontrol system used in an oil pipeline monitoring application. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A geostationary satellite at 36,000 km altitude transmits telemetry data to an Earth station at a carrier frequency of 4 GHz using an EIRP of 20 dBW. The ground station has a G/T of 30 dB/K. Compute (i) the free-space path loss in dB, (ii) the received C/N₀, and (iii) the maximum achievable data rate for BPSK with required Eb/N₀ of 9.6 dB. Assume k = -228.6 dBW/K/Hz and neglect atmospheric losses. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

The block diagram was given in **Section 4.4** of these notes. The verbal explanation should cover:

1. **Field-side sensors (Pumping stations, valve stations, delivery terminals):**
   - Pressure transducers measure line pressure to detect leaks (sudden drop) or blockages (sudden rise).
   - Coriolis flow meters measure mass flow rate.
   - RTDs measure product temperature.
   - Cathodic-protection voltage sensors detect pipeline corrosion.

2. **Signal conditioning and ADC:**
   - Sensor outputs (4–20 mA or 0–10 V) are normalized, filtered, and digitized (typically 12-bit ADC, with $\text{SQNR} \approx 74$ dB).
   - PCM encoding converts samples into binary words (NRZ-L format).

3. **Communication channel (multiple options):**
   - **Primary:** Licensed UHF radio (450–470 MHz) with directional Yagi antennas and intermediate repeater towers (every 30–50 km).
   - **Backup:** Satellite (VSAT) in Ku-band (12–14 GHz) for redundant long-distance links.
   - **Tertiary:** Cellular 4G/5G or leased fiber along the pipeline right-of-way.

4. **Master station / SCADA:**
   - Receives, decodes, displays, and logs all telemetry data.
   - Generates alarms on out-of-limit values (e.g., pressure drop > 2 bar/min triggers leak alarm).
   - Stores data in a historian database for audit and analysis.

5. **Telecontrol path:**
   - Operator initiates commands (open/close valve, start/stop pump) via HMI.
   - Command is encoded, modulated, and transmitted back to the field.
   - Actuator driver (solenoid valve, VFD) executes the command.
   - Confirmation telemetry is returned for "command executed" feedback.

### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Given data:**
- $d = 36{,}000$ km
- $f = 4$ GHz
- EIRP = 20 dBW
- $G/T = 30$ dB/K
- $k = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz
- $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB

**Step 2 — Compute FSPL:**

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(36{,}000) + 20 \log_{10}(4000)
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(36{,}000) = 91.13 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(4000) = 72.04 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 91.13 + 72.04 = 195.61 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C/N_0$:**

$$
\frac{C}{N_0} = \text{EIRP} + \frac{G}{T} - k - \text{FSPL} = 20 + 30 - (-228.6) - 195.61
$$

$$
\frac{C}{N_0} = 20 + 30 + 228.6 - 195.61 = 82.99 \text{ dB-Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute max data rate:**

$$
10 \log_{10}(R_b) = \frac{C}{N_0} - \frac{E_b}{N_0} = 82.99 - 9.6 = 73.39
$$

$$
R_b = 10^{7.339} \approx 2.18 \times 10^{7} \text{ bps} \approx 21.8 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> **Answer:** FSPL ≈ **195.6 dB**, $C/N_0$ ≈ **83 dB-Hz**, max data rate ≈ **21.8 Mbps**.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**
> **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Model Paper 1, Q8]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, describe the telemetry, tracking, and command (TT&C) subsystem of a deep-space probe mission (e.g., Mars Orbiter Mission). Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute the one-way and round-trip propagation delays when the same probe is (i) at closest approach to Earth (54.6 million km), and (ii) on the opposite side of the Sun from Earth (~ 401 million km). Comment on the implications for telecontrol of the spacecraft. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

The block diagram was given in **Section 4.2** of these notes. Verbal explanation:

1. **On-board TT&C hardware:**
   - **Telemetry section:** Sensors measure temperatures, voltages, currents, attitude (star tracker, sun sensor, IMU), propulsion tank pressure, etc. On-board computer formats data into **CCSDS source packets**, packs them into a **Transfer Frame**, and applies channel coding (e.g., convolutional rate 1/2, K=7, with optional Reed-Solomon outer code for further protection).
   - **Modulator & HPA:** BPSK or QPSK modulation, then up-conversion to S-band (2.2 GHz) and amplification by a **Solid-State Power Amplifier (SSPA)** of typically 5–60 W. The antenna is usually a **low-gain omni** (for safe mode) plus a **medium-gain horn** (for normal operations). High-gain parabolic dish is used for high-rate data at Mars.
   - **Command receiver:** A separate LNA + coherent receiver chain (often a redundant pair) listens on the uplink frequency. The received command is demodulated, decoded, and verified by the on-board **Command Decoder Unit (CDU)** before execution.

2. **Ground segment (DSN):**
   - **Antennas:** 34-m beam-waveguide antennas (primary) and 70-m antennas (for high-elevation, weak-signal deep-space phases).
   - **Front-end:** Cryogenically cooled **maser amplifier** (~ 4 K system temperature at S-band, ~ 25 K at Ka-band) followed by downconversion and digital sampling.
   - **Signal processing:** Viterbi/sequential decoder for convolutional; Reed-Solomon decoder for outer code. The decoded telemetry frames are forwarded to the **Mission Operations Center (MOC)** via a dedicated WAN.
   - **Telecommand uplink:** Commands are encoded, modulated (typically PCM/PSK/PM on a subcarrier), and uplinked through the same DSN antenna. **Ranging** is achieved by sending a known pseudo-random code and measuring the round-trip phase.

3. **Tracking:**
   - Two-way **Doppler** (spacecraft coherent transponder retransmits the uplink carrier; ground measures frequency shift → line-of-sight velocity).
   - Two-way **ranging** (PN code turnaround → line-of-sight distance).
   - **Δ-DOR (Differential One-Way Ranging)** using two widely separated DSN stations for angular position in the plane of sky.

### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Step 1 — At closest approach (54.6 × 10⁶ km):**

$$
t_{\text{one-way}} = \frac{54.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ km}}{3 \times 10^{5} \text{ km/s}} = 182 \text{ s} \approx 3.03 \text{ min}
$$

$$
t_{\text{RT}} = 2 \times 182 = 364 \text{ s} \approx 6.07 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — At conjunction (~ 401 × 10⁶ km):**

$$
t_{\text{one-way}} = \frac{401 \times 10^{6}}{3 \times 10^{5}} = 1336.7 \text{ s} \approx 22.3 \text{ min}
$$

$$
t_{\text{RT}} = 2 \times 1336.7 = 2673.3 \text{ s} \approx 44.6 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Implications for telecontrol:**

- **No real-time closed-loop control** is possible. Even at closest approach, a 6-minute round-trip means that the spacecraft state has changed by the time a corrective command arrives. The controller must be *open-loop predictive*: a sequence of commands is uploaded and executed autonomously.
- **At conjunction**, the 44-minute round-trip further constrains operations; emergency fault response could take ~1.5 hours to be confirmed, so on-board **Fault Detection, Isolation, and Recovery (FDIR)** autonomy is essential.
- **Communication windows** are scheduled and pre-verified; out-of-window anomalies must be handled by the spacecraft's autonomous software (e.g., ISRO's MOM "safemode" and NASA JPL's AutoNav).
- **Solar conjunction** (Sun between Earth and Mars) also introduces solar corona plasma noise, which corrupts the RF link. During such periods (typically 2 weeks), communications are reduced to only carrier-tone "heartbeat" signals to confirm spacecraft survival.

> **Answer:** 3.03 min / 6.07 min at closest; 22.3 min / 44.6 min at farthest. Real-time telecontrol is impossible; **autonomous on-board FDIR and command sequencing are mandatory**.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Telemetry Scheme Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing EIRP with transmitter power.** EIRP = $P_{\text{Tx}} + G_{\text{Tx}} - L_{\text{feed}}$. Many students write $P_{\text{Tx}}$ directly into the link equation, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Wrong units in the log form of FSPL.** The constant 32.44 is valid only when distance is in **km** and frequency is in **MHz**. Using m and Hz gives a wildly different constant (and an incorrect answer).
> 3. **Omitting the Boltzmann constant $k$ in the C/N₀ formula.** Some students write $C/N$ (a dimensionless ratio) instead of $C/N_0$ (in Hz), missing the $-228.6$ dB term.
> 4. **Drawing the block diagram but not labeling each block's function.** This typically costs 2–3 marks out of 7 in part (a) of a 14-mark question.
> 5. **Using $|x|$ (vertical pipe) in markdown table formulas.** The markdown parser interprets the pipe as a column separator, breaking the table. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX.
> 6. **Confusing S-band, C-band, X-band, Ku-band, Ka-band frequencies.** Memorize: S = 2–4 GHz, C = 4–8 GHz, X = 8–12 GHz, Ku = 12–18 GHz, Ka = 26–40 GHz.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert round-trip time into closed-loop implications.** When asked about Mars delay, the follow-up "comment on telecontrol" is mandatory — examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically for the *implications*, not just the numerical answer.
> 8. **Spelling telecommand as "tele-command" or "tele command".** Use the single-word spelling.
> 9. **Neglecting to mention redundancy** when describing spacecraft TT&C. DSN and spacecraft both maintain command receiver *redundancy* (A-string + B-string) and an omni-antenna for emergencies. This is a 1-mark "polish" item examiners often award.
> 10. **Mixing up CCSDS and IRIG standards.** CCSDS is for *space*; IRIG-106 is for *aerospace and industrial* test ranges. Do not interchange.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-density):**

- **Telemetry** = measure remotely; **Telecontrol** = act remotely. Both are essential for any non-attended system.
- **Five canonical blocks** of any telemetry chain: **DAQ → Encoder → Tx → Channel → Rx → Display/Storage**.
- **Industrial schemes** — pipeline, power substation, manufacturing plant, water treatment. Use **wired + wireless** hybrid, **SCADA** as supervisory layer, **IEC 61850 / Modbus / DNP3 / OPC-UA** as protocols.
- **Space schemes** — LEO satellites, GEO satellites, deep-space probes, manned missions. Use **CCSDS** standards, **S/X/Ka-bands**, **DSN** for deep space, **TDRS** for LEO/GEO.
- **Distance hierarchy:** Plant (m–km) < LEO (200–2000 km) < GEO (36,000 km) < Lunar (384,000 km) < Mars (54.6–401 × 10⁶ km) < Voyager (24 × 10⁹ km).
- **Time delay hierarchy:** Plant (< 1 ms) < LEO (< 10 ms) < GEO (~ 240 ms) < Lunar (~ 1.3 s) < Mars (3–22 min) < Voyager (> 22 hr).
- **Band frequency memory hook — "S = 2, X = 8, K = 26+":** S-band 2 GHz, X-band 8 GHz, Ka-band 26+ GHz.
- **FSPL formula in dB:** $\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$.
- **GEO S-band FSPL ≈ 190 dB**; GEO Ka-band FSPL ≈ **205 dB** (15 dB higher per ~16× frequency).
- **Boltzmann constant in dB:** $k = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz. **Always include** in $C/N_0$ link budgets.
- **Mars round-trip delay** is 6–44 minutes — this is the *single most important number* for Mars mission design.
- **CCSDS = space data standard; IRIG-106 = industrial/range standard.** Don't mix them.
- **Power system telemetry uses PMU** for synchrophasors at 30–120 samples/sec.
- **Pipeline telemetry uses UHF radio + satellite VSAT + cellular** as a three-tier redundancy.
- **DSN has three 120°-separated sites** for continuous deep-space coverage.
- **TT&C = Telemetry + Tracking + Command.** Memorize this acronym.
- **The Shannon limit** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bounds the maximum data rate; modern coding (LDPC, turbo) approaches within 0.5 dB of it.
- **Nyquist** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ is the floor for PCM sampling in any digital telemetry system.
- **PCM SQNR** = $6.02N + 1.76$ dB for sine input; $6.02N - 7.27$ dB for Gaussian.
- **For closed-loop stability under telecontrol delay**, use Routh–Hurwitz or Bode margins; a rough rule is **controller gain × delay < 0.5 × process time constant** for well-damped response.
- **Voyager signal at Earth** is ~ $10^{-22}$ W/m² — about 20 billion times weaker than a digital watch battery's signal at 1 m. This is why deep-space antennas are 70 m and amplifiers are cryogenically cooled masers.
- **Autonomy is mandatory** for any mission with round-trip delay exceeding 1 second — the spacecraft must be able to survive and recover from faults *without* ground intervention.
- **Industry 4.0** trends: IoT sensors + MQTT/OPC-UA + cloud SCADA + 5G/TSN networks.
- **Standards to remember by name:** IRIG-106, CCSDS, Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850, OPC-UA, MQTT, WirelessHART, ISA100.11a, PROFINET, EtherCAT.
- **For exam diagrams, always label every block with a one-line function.** A diagram without labels is worth only ~3/7 marks in a 7-mark part.
